cse1_name: HaqCheck’s fact-checking training series
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: HaqCheck’s fact-checking training series (held across Ethiopia in Addis Ababa Dire Dawa and Arba Minch from 7 Mar–24 Apr 2024) hosted through the HaqCheck fact-checking initiative (in collaboration with Addis Ababa University and supported by the U.S. Embassy) trained journalists bloggers and other participants on digital/media literacy OSINT and verification techniques to spot and debunk AI-enabled misinformation including “deepfake/AI” manipulated audio-visual content. By strengthening early detection documentation fact-checking and public correction it helps reduce the spread of harmful narratives that can escalate tensions or trigger violence.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: Legal Challenge Against the French Family Welfare Scoring Algorithm
cse1_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse1_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse1_justif: The legal challenge directly addresses Fairness and Non-discrimination by arguing the CNAF (Caisse Nationale des Allocations Familiales - National Family Allowance Fund) algorithm is 'discriminatory by design' targeting vulnerable groups like single mothers and disabled people equating poverty with suspicion. It addresses Transparency by demanding the algorithm's code and function be made public challenging its opacity. It also relates to Human Oversight by contesting a system that automates suspicion based on opaque scores potentially diminishing human judgment in deciding who gets investigated.
cse2_name: Objectif IA: Get Started with Artificial Intelligence
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Environmental Impact;  Transparency and Explainability
cse2_justif: This free course addresses Environmental Impact in Part 2 Chapter 3 which discusses the energy consumption of data centers and the ecological footprint of training algorithms. It also targets Transparency and Explainability in Part 2 Chapter 2  which covers the challenges of explaining black-box models and ensuring safety. Fairness and Non-discrimination are also addressed in Part 2 Chapter 3 specifically through sections on detecting and reducing algorithmic bias. (Start date: https://web.archive.org/web/20260103160446/https://www.institutmontaigne.org/publications/objectif-ia)
cse3_name: Conference: Can AI be frugal?
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_justif: This conference organized by the professional association NWX (Normandie Web Xperts) on Feb 15 2024 featured Thomas Solignac discussing 'frugal AI.' It aimed to raise awareness among approximately 100 attendees about AI's environmental impact including energy consumption and resource extraction. Discussions covered measuring impacts best practices (like AFNOR standards) regulation and low-tech AI directly engaging professionals on the Sustainability of AI.
cse4_name: At France Travail the rise of algorithmic control
cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse4_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Human Oversight and Determination;  Transparency and Explainability
cse4_justif: This investigative article by La Quadrature du Net published June 25 2024 reveals the use of opaque algorithms by France Travail to 'evaluate sort and classify' job seekers. It addresses Transparency by exposing these hidden systems. It tackles Human Oversight by detailing the 'dehumanization of social support' due to automation. It relates to Fairness by highlighting how these systems facilitate control and risk stigmatizing precarious individuals.
cse5_name: Labelia - Responsible and Trustworthy AI Certification
cse5_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse5_contributions: Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability
cse5_justif: This initiative by the non-profit Labelia provides certification for 'Responsible and Trustworthy AI' described as the first of its kind in Europe. It includes a reference framework structured around 5 axes: Ethics & Societal Impact (addressing Fairness) Communication & Transparency (addressing Transparency) and Management & Governance (addressing Human Oversight). It offers an assessment platform and certification audit to help organizations demonstrate compliance and ethical practices.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: Legal challenge against the CNAF's discriminatory welfare algorithm
cse1_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse1_justif: In October 2024 a coalition of 15 Civil Society Organisations (CSOs) led by La Quadrature du Net filed litigation before the Council of State against the CNAF (Caisse Nationale des Allocations Familiales - National Family Allowance Fund). The coalition which includes Amnesty International France challenges the CNAF's use of an algorithmic risk-scoring system to detect fraud. They argue the algorithm is discriminatory as it disproportionately targets vulnerable populations such as single mothers people with disabilities and low-income households by using socio-economic data as proxies for 'risk' (p. 1). This action addresses inclusion and diversity by seeking to protect marginalised groups from automated discrimination (p. 2).
cse2_name: DéClics Numériques: 20th edition focused on Artificial Intelligence
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse2_justif: This initiative the 20th edition of 'DéClics Numériques' focused on AI  was organized by the CSO Diversidays. It addresses Cultural and Linguistic Diversity (in the broad sense of social inclusion) by promoting equal opportunities for underrepresented talents regardless of their social cultural or geographical origins to access training and jobs in the digital/AI sector. The program aims to facilitate professional retraining into AI-related careers for these diverse groups fostering a more inclusive workforce.
cse3_name: Participation in the LINGUATEC-IA project
cse3_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse3_justif: This initiative the LINGUATEC-IA project (January 1 2024 - December 31 2026) involves the CSO Lo Congrès permanent de la lenga occitana as a partner. It constitutes knowledge generation addressing Cultural and Linguistic Diversity. Its explicit goal is to advance the digitization of the POCTEFA territory languages (Aragonese Catalan Basque and Occitan) using Artificial Intelligence. The project develops AI knowledge and tools like neural language models machine translation and voice synthesis specifically tailored for these less-resourced languages thereby contributing to their digital presence and vitality.
cse4_name: Launch of a working group on the use of AI in child protection
cse4_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse4_contributions: Children’s Rights
cse4_justif: The Convention Nationale des Associations de Protection de l'Enfant (CNAPE - National Convention of Child Protection Associations) launched this working group to address AI's impact on child protection covering risks like profiling and cyber-pedocriminality.Its goals are to explore AI use cases support ethical practices respectful of children's rights and develop sector-specific recommendations for public authorities. This directly contributes to protecting the rights and fundamental needs of children in the age of AI.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: Report of the Central Social and Economic Committee of July 24 2025: France Travail of the Future or the Generalisation of Artificial Intelligence
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: The General Confederation of Labour union within the Central Social and Economic Committee of the public agency France Travail evaluated the deployment of Artificial Intelligence tools (such as the virtual advisor NEO). The report identifies grave risks including psychosocial risks anxiety at work and loss of skills for agents. Citing an audit by the expert firm Degest the union challenges the administration's efficiency plan and explicitly demands the opening of negotiations to regulate Artificial Intelligence use to protect jobs and working conditions.
cse2_name: AI Initiation Workshops
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives
cse2_justif: Simplon a social enterprise partnered with Meta and the Impact AI collective to launch 'AI Initiation Workshops' (Page 1). The initiative aimed to train 30000 French people in generative AI by the end of 2024 (Page 2). It specifically aimed to make digital skills accessible to all including those 'far from employment or undergoing professional retraining' (Page 2). The program includes half-day workshops in eight cities and an online MOOC to ensure broad access (Page 2) while raising awareness of ethical issues and responsible use (Page 3).
cse3_name: General Union of Engineers Executives and Technicians: Study Day - Artificial Intelligence: What Future for Skilled Work?
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_justif: The General Union of Engineers Executives and Technicians of the General Confederation of Labour organized a thematic study day titled 'Artificial Intelligence: What future for skilled work?' on April 26 2024 (Page 1). The event aimed to raise awareness about the impact of Artificial Intelligence on skilled jobs. The program included roundtables on topics such as 'Help my colleague is an algorithm?' and 'Trade union intelligence against the worst of Artificial Intelligence?' (Page 3) as well as a forward-looking exhibition by the Association for the Employment of Executives on the future of work (Page 3).
cse4_name: Charter 'Algorithms Artificial Intelligence and Recruitment'
cse4_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse4_contributions: Labour Protections
cse4_justif: The association 'With Equal Skills' (À Compétence Égale) defined a charter for users of algorithmic systems in the context of identification evaluation or selection of job applicants (Page 13 source 965). The initiative aims to guarantee respect for the rights of job applicants and fight against discrimination in recruitment processes involving Artificial Intelligence (Page 13 source 964-965). This addresses Labour Protections by setting ethical standards for recruiters to prevent automated bias.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Generative AI Committee (Comité de l’intelligence artificielle générative)
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: This advisory council (Sept 2023–Mar 2024) integrated civil society (e.g. CFDT Union researchers) into France's AI policy formulation. Its mandate was to define a national strategy for Generative AI to 'enlighten the Government's decisions.' The final report directly addresses Safety and Security by recommending adapted regulations to 'protect against abuses' (dérives) and Impact Assessments by evaluating the technology's consequences on labor markets culture and society. This demonstrates active CSO partnership with the state to shape governance. https://web.archive.org/web/20260115182647/https://www.info.gouv.fr/upload/media/content/0001/09/4d3cc456dd2f5b9d79ee75feea63b47f10d75158.pdf
cse2_name: Complaint against algorithmic video surveillance processing implemented by the police prefecture
cse2_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse2_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Access to Redress and Remedy;  Safety and Security
cse2_justif: On July 31 2024 the CSO La Quadrature du Net filed a formal complaint with the CNIL against the Police Prefecture regarding the use of algorithmic video surveillance (VSA) for the 2024 Olympics. The initiative directly addresses Data Protection by alleging that the deployment violated the Data Protection Act (Loi Informatique et Libertés). It also addresses Access to Redress and Remedy by arguing that the late publication of authorization decrees (one day before implementation) hindered citizens' fundamental 'right to an effective remedy' (droit à un recours effectif).
cse3_name: Petition campaign: France must not become the champion of surveillance
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Safety and Security
cse3_justif: Amnesty International France launched this petition to oppose Algorithmic Video Surveillance (VSA) legalised for the 2024 Olympics. It addresses Data Protection and Privacy by criticising how VSA analyzes behavioral data in public spaces which threatens the right to anonymity. It also highlights Safety and Security implications warning that these 'exceptional' security measures risk normalising mass surveillance and discriminatory practices paving the way for facial recognition technologies that undermine civil liberties.
cse4_name: Operationalizing AI System Risk Management
cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse4_contributions: Safety and Security;  Impact Assessments
cse4_justif: On July 19 2024 the non-profit association Hub France IA published a technical guide titled 'Operationalizing AI System Risk Management.' This deliverable addresses Safety and Security by providing organizations with a practical framework to map and mitigate risks associated with AI systems ensuring compliance with the upcoming EU AI Act. It specifically covers 'High-Risk AI Systems' and generative AI offering tools to evaluate technical and operational risks which directly contributes to the implementation of robust Impact Assessments in the French ecosystem.
cse5_name: Paris Charter on AI and Journalism
cse5_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse5_contributions: AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence;  Safety and Security
cse5_justif: Reporters Without Borders (RSF) in partnership with 16 international CSOs launched the 'Paris Charter' in November 2023 to regulate the use of AI in media. The initiative directly addresses AI-facilitated Misinformation by establishing 10 ethical principles such as the requirement to clearly distinguish between human and AI-generated content and ensuring human oversight in editorial decisions. It serves as a 'best practice' guide for newsrooms to maintain the integrity of information and protect democratic deliberation from the risks of AI-driven disinformation.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: Gambian Educators Trained on Artificial Intelligence in the Classroom
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: Africa Missions Global's (AMG) Gambia chapter in partnership with the Ministry of Basic and Secondary Education facilitated a training workshop for over 200 teachers administrators and principles focused on the use of Artificial Intelligence (AI) in education and preparing teachers for the future of education. The session included interactive presentations and real-life examples that helped demystify AI for educators who may have had little prior exposure. They also addressed ethical considerations in AI use and stressed the need for responsible integration in education systems.
cse2_name: Conference on the impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on journalism
cse2_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse2_contributions: Labour Protections
cse2_justif: Led by the Gambia Press Union (GPU) a journalists’ trade union and civil society organisation media professionals government officials and civil society leaders convened at the Bakadaji Hotel in Kololi to mark this year’s World Press Freedom Day. The day focused on the transformative yet perilous impact of Artificial Intelligence (AI) on journalism. Speakers discussed AI’s impact on journalism including job displacement and working conditions for media workers and raised public awareness of risks that automation and AI pose to media labour.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: AI Week for women and girls to take place in Georgia
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: The initiative aims to demolish the gender gap in technology and science by fostering a new generation of highly skilled women in the field. The goal of the project is to equip women and girls with the necessary skills to integrate contemporary technologies into everyday life. Fifteen female entrepreneurs will facilitate sessions for fifteen female students from grades 9-12. The project will be executed through a series of workshops and practical exercises over the mentioned span of five days.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: AI and...  Series of Lectures
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_contributions: AI Literacy;  Reskilling and upskilling initiatives
cse1_justif: This lecture series organised by the Georgian AI Association covers a range of topics from different fields and features discussions with experts on how AI can be integrated into their respective domains what impact it might have and related issues. The series is held on a recurring monthly basis and is open to the public. So this initiative addresses the topic of reskilling and upskilling

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: Advisory Board of the Digital Services Coordinator
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: The Advisory Board is a multi-stakeholder body composed of 16 representatives from academia private sector and civil society (e.g. AlgorithmWatch HateAid LOAD e.v. Federation of German Consumer Organisations etc.). The board advises the Digital Services Coordinator (DSC) on how to audit the recommender systems and content moderation algorithms of platforms to ensure they do not produce discriminatory outcomes or bias against specific groups. They specifically review compliance with Art. 34 of the Digital Services Act (DSA). The board facilitates Data Access Requests (Art. 40 DSA). DSC's also overlook 'trusted flaggers' -- independent experts detect and remove illegal content (https://digital-strategy.ec.europa.eu/en/policies/dsa-dscs)
cse2_name: Let's Stop Nudification Apps Together!
cse2_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse2_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse2_justif: This initiative leverages the Digital Services Act (DSA) to combat systemic gender-based violence. Non-consensual AI-generated imagery ('nudification') disproportionately targets and harms women and girls reinforcing deep-seated social inequalities. By forcing platforms like X and Meta to mitigate these risks under Articles 34 and 35 AlgorithmWatch initiates lawsuits in the aim to eliminate a discriminatory 'safety gap' where gendered harassment is often less strictly moderated than other illegal content. Furthermore using Article 40 to demand data access aims to uncover algorithmic biases in how these harmful tools are promoted ensuring that marginalised groups receive equal protection from AI-facilitated abuse.
cse3_name: Within Bounds: Limiting AI's environmental impact: Joint statement from civil society for the AI Action Summit
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_justif: The joint statement led by the Green Screen Coalition a group coalition of funders and practitioners addresses AI’s environmental impact by demanding that computational infrastructure operates within 'planetary boundaries' (Intro). It warns that data centre electricity use may reach 1000 TWh by 2026 (Sec 1). To counter this the initiative calls for a fossil fuel phase-out and 100% renewable energy (Sec 1). Section II addresses resource exhaustion noting that data centers consume millions of liters of water daily. Finally Section 5 mandates tracking energy and water use across the AI lifecycle to ensure transparency and accountability moving beyond false 'climate solutions' to verifiable ecological limits.
cse4_name: Report algorithmic discrimination!
cse4_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse4_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse4_justif: This initiative led by the German CSO AlgorithmWatch directly addresses fairness and non-discrimination by creating a structured channel to identify and document cases of potential algorithmic discrimination. By inviting individuals to report suspected harms it helps uncover how algorithmic systems may produce unequal or biased outcomes in practice. The collected reports contribute to making discriminatory effects more visible and better understood thereby generating evidence on systemic fairness issues. This in turn supports AlgorithmWatch’s advocacy for stronger protections accountability and safeguards against discriminatory uses of AI reinforcing efforts to promote equitable and non-discriminatory AI.
cse5_name: Joint Statement: Upcoming Commission Guidelines on the AI Act Implementation: Human Rights and Justice Must Be at Their Heart
cse5_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse5_justif: AlgorithmWatch (Germany-based CSO) contributes to this joint civil society statement advocating for ethical guidelines in their rights-based implementation of the EU AI Act. The document explicitly addresses fairness and non-discrimination by highlighting how AI systems (e.g. social scoring predictive policing biometric categorisation) can disproportionately harm marginalised groups including racialised communities migrants and persons with disabilities. It calls for broad interpretation of prohibited practices and safeguards against proxy discrimination (e.g. postcode data).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: Hamburg Declaration on Responsible AI for the SDGs
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Cultural and Linguistic Diversity;  Gender Equality;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities
cse1_justif: Concluded at the Hamburg Sustainability Conference 2025 this initiative involved CSOs working with the German government in drafting principles for 'equitable inclusive and sustainable' AI deployment. It specifically targets the integration of underrepresented languages and diverse cultural contexts into AI systems (p.2-3) and ensures the representation of individuals with disabilities (p.3) in AI ethics conferences. Signatories committed to promoting gender equality (p. 3-4) and inclusivity by ensuring that AI systems are developed through inclusive participation particularly from the Global South and marginalized communities (p. 3&5) and the underrepresentation of diverse cultural and linguistic groups in AI training data (p.4).
cse2_name: AI & Society Lab (Alexander von Humboldt Institute for Internet and Society (HIIG))
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Gender Equality;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse2_justif: The AI & Society Lab hosts workshop series on'Public Interest AI' (p.7). During the 2023–2025 period their work has directly addressed several key dimensions of inclusion. The Simba project (p. 12) is an open-source AI-powered tool that provides automated text simplification for those with disabilities and promotes inclusion of non-native German speakers. The Mapping of Public Interest AI pilot program (p.11-12) focused on linguistic diversity. The goal is to ensure models are trained in local languages to capture cultural nuances that English-only models miss. The 'Women* in Tech' project aims to strengthen the equal rights of women worldwide through networking and expertise as well as to create new opportunities (p. 6).
cse3_name: AI's Missing Link: The Gender Gap in the Talent Pool (Interface)
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_justif: Interface published an extensive data-driven analysis of the gender gap within the global and European AI workforce. The research highlights that Germany lags significantly behind other EU nations (p.4 p.18 p.31) with women comprising only approximately 20% of AI professionals. The report argues that this homogeneity leads to AI systems that reflect and amplify societal biases (p.5). The goal of the report is to provide policymakers with the evidence needed to develop strategies for closing the gender gap emphasising that diversity is not just an equality issue but a prerequisite for 'developing AI systems that work equitably for all of society' (p.5).
cse4_name: Joint statement: Upcoming Commission Guidelines on the AI Act Implementation: Human Rights and Justice Must Be at Their Heart
cse4_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse4_contributions: Gender Equality;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse4_justif: AlgorithmWatch (Germany-based CSO) leads this civil society statement on the implementation of the EU AI Act advocating for ethical guidelines in their rights-based AI governance. The document explicitly addresses cultural and linguistic diversity and anti-racism by highlighting risks of AI systems reinforcing discrimination against racialised and migrant communities (e.g. social scoring predictive policing). It also covers gender equality and disability rights through its call to regulate biometric categorisation and proxy data that can infer sensitive attributes such as gender identity ethnicity or disability. By demanding broad interpretation of harms and stronger safeguards it advances inclusive AI principles

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: DGB (German Trade Union Confederation) position paper to the German Federal Goverment's Digital Affairs Committee on 'National leeway in implementation of European law on AI'
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_justif: The DGB (German Trade Union Confederation) was part of the public expert hearings of the German federal government's Committee on Digital Affairs (link in 'open observations'). The DGB also provided a formal written submission to the German federal government's Committee on Digital Affairs on how Germany should translate the EU AI Act into national law especially within the context of employment context – an area that the group claims the EU AI act deliberately leaves to the Member States (p. 1 para 5). The DGB paper cites Art. 2 Para. 11 of the AI Act as the primary legal gateway for national labor law (p.2) demands for a specialised employee data protection law (p.3) and demands to exclude Predictive Analytics in the workplace (p.3).
cse2_name: Free training for Berlin educators to learn how to open critical conversations about the impacts of AI & tech (Tactical Tech)
cse2_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse2_contributions: AI Literacy
cse2_justif: Tactical Tech is an international non-profit and hosted a workshop targeted specifically at Berlin-based educators. The open call page description states that the event will use a training-of-trainers format on AI misinformation and digital literacy ('What does partnership look like?') and offers funding for participants’ activities in local communities ('Goals of the training'). This initiative promotes AI literacy by equipping teachers to recognise AI-generated content explain risks and safeguards to pupils and cascade practical classroom resources.
cse3_drive: Joint statement: Upcoming Commission Guidelines on the AI Act Implementation: Human Rights and Justice Must Be at Their Heart
cse3_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse3_justif: AlgorithmWatch (Germany-based CSO) contributes to this joint civil society statement advocating for ethical guidelines in their rights-based implementation of the EU AI Act. The document explicitly addresses labour protections by noting AI use in employment contexts where social scoring practices are prevalent and should be prohibited

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Digital Firewall (Chaos Computer Club)
cse1_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse1_justif: During the study period the German NGO Chaos Computer Club (CCC) actively served as an expert advisor and participated in parliamentary hearings and public consultations regarding the German Digital Violence Protection Act (Digitale-Gewalt-Schutz-Gesetz). On June 24 2024 the CCC provided formal testimony to the Committee on Internal Affairs regarding the amendment of the Federal Data Protection Act (BDSG) arguing against the legal expansion of biometric surveillance. On March 6 2025 CCC presented a 'Digital Firewall' position paper to policy-makers to ban biometric mass surveillance and automated profiling directly influencing the implementation of the EU AI Act in Germany and the data protection and privacy of German citizens.
cse2_name: Report Algorithmic Discrimination! (AlgorithmWatch)
cse2_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse2_contributions: Consumer Protection;  Impact Assessments;  Access to Redress and Remedy
cse2_justif: The 'Report Algorithmic Discrimination!' project systematically documents suspected cases of AI bias in hiring and credit scoring to build strategic litigation. This challenges the 'black box' barrier that often prevents individuals from proving discrimination under the German General Equal Treatment Act (AGG). Furthermore it integrates with AlgorithmWatch’s Impact Assessment Tool  which provides a two-stage impact assessment procedure (Triage and Transparency Report) to help organisations identify ethically relevant risks before deployment. By exposing systematically biased 'proxy variables' the initiative holds proprietary algorithms accountable for discriminatory data assessments.
cse3_name: Impact Assessment Tool for Responsible AI (AlgorithmWatch)
cse3_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse3_justif: AlgorithmWatch’s tool operationalises Impact Assessments through a two-stage process of Triage and Transparency Reporting. The Triage stage filters systems by risk level while the Transparency Report requires detailed documentation of goals performance metrics and accountability structures.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: AI Learning for young people in Africa
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_justif: The AI Literacy initiative addresses the growing need for AI education as a core component of modern literacy. This program aims to equip children educators and communities with essential skills to navigate and thrive in an AI-driven world promoting a new era of digital inclusion and ethical AI use. Initial rollout is in select schools across GHANA Tanzania and Nigeria with plans for expansion to other African countries by 2026.
cse2_name: Eighth Quadrennial Delegates Conference of the Communication Workers Union Ho. Theme: 'The Impact of Artificial Intelligence on the Future of Work: The Role of the Union'
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives
cse2_justif: The conference addressed the reskilling and upskilling indicator primarily at the agenda-setting and advocacy level.Conference discussions highlighted AI-driven automation as a risk to employment and emphasised the need for proactive worker reskilling and upskilling through education retraining and skills development. The conference framed skills transition as a shared responsibility of unions government and industry actors.While no specific reskilling programmes or training initiatives were announced the conference contributed to the reskilling and upskilling indicator by embedding workforce skills transition within union policy discourse and advocacy rather than through direct implementation and actionable measures.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Ghana Fact: Ghana's Independent Fact-checking platform
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: Ghana Fact addresses AI-facilitated misinformation and violence by detecting verifying and debunking AI-generated false content such as deepfakes and manipulated media. As Ghana’s IFCN-verified fact-checking platform it counters the spread of harmful narratives that could incite hate or violence. Through media literacy campaigns and public education it strengthens citizens’ capacity to identify false information promoting safer digital spaces and reducing the societal risks of AI-driven misinformation. The evidence can be read as technical report as the link provided directs to a list of all the fact-checked cases many of them including AI deepfakes

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: Homo Digitalis's 4th Study on the AI ACT  - Comparative overview of the provisions on the rights of affected individuals
cse1_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse1_justif: The Homo Digitalis initiative '4th Study on the AI Act – Comparative overview of the provisions on the rights of affected individuals' scrutinises the right to explanation through a detailed legal comparison of Article 86 of the AI Act and Article 22 of the GDPR (p. 20–21). It analyses key legal terms and interpretive gaps and evaluates how both frameworks enable human oversight and redress. The study proposes concrete improvements: defining 'clear' and 'substantial' explanations specifying 'main elements' of decisions technical criteria and stronger supervisory mechanisms to detect bias and discrimination (p. 24).
cse2_name: FOIA before the Greek Ministry of Education for its Memorandum of Cooperation with OpenAI
cse2_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse2_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse2_justif: The initiative by Homo Digitalis is a legal request to the Ministry of Education regarding the 'OpenAI for Greece' program which addresses fairness transparency and human oversight by invoking Articles 4-8 of Law 4961/2022. It requests disclosure of impact assessments transparency obligations and safeguards for students’ rights in the use of AI systems in public education. Through legal advocacy it promotes accountability and compliance with human rights and data protection standards in AI deployment.
cse3_name: InfoLaw 2024 Conference - “Regulating Digital Innovation: Artificial Intelligence Cybersecurity and Platforms”
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_contributions: Human Oversight and Determination;  Transparency and Explainability;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse3_justif: The InfoLaw 2024 Conference examines the legal and social implications of AI cybersecurity and online platforms. Its first session on the AI Act discusses human oversight general purpose AI and fundamental rights impact assessments (FRIA) directly addressing transparency explainability and fairness. A workshop on “AI and Justice” further explores ethical and supervisory aspects of AI use in the legal field.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: The Greek CSO Homo Digitalis participated in the AI Office’s public consultation on Prohibited Practices under the EU Artificial Intelligence Act.
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_contributions: Gender Equality;  Children’s Rights;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse1_justif: The Homo Digitalis contribution to the EC consultation (Dec 2024) addresses gender equality children’s rights disability inclusion and cultural and linguistic diversity. It calls for stricter prohibitions under Article 5 of the AI Act against systems that reinforce stereotypes exploit vulnerabilities or discriminate against marginalised groups (p. 19 20 28). On p. 33 it explicitly supports a ban on emotion-recognition technologies arguing they are unreliable invasive and ignore cultural and contextual differences in emotional expression. It urges a broad ban covering proxies like “suspicious” or “untrustworthy” in contexts with power imbalances such as policing and migration.
cse2_name: Greek CSO SciFY’s Initiative on Disability Inclusion in AI  - “People with Disabilities (PWD) Pioneers in Artificial Intelligence”
cse2_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse2_contributions: Rights of Persons with Disabilities
cse2_justif: The SciFY “PwD Pioneers in AI” initiative promotes social inclusion and equal opportunities for persons with disabilities by integrating them into the AI development process. Through training programs participants with autism and mental health conditions learn to perform AI data annotation (text sound image) proving their ability to contribute meaningfully to AI innovation. The project challenges social and cultural stereotypes about disability and highlights how AI can serve as a tool for empowerment accessibility and inclusion rather than exclusion.
cse3_name: FRIA - Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment Guideline
cse3_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse3_contributions: Gender Equality;  Children’s Rights;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse3_justif: The FRIA template constitutes a test for a comprehensive legal compliance assessment of AI systems which is becoming necessary for many systems including those classified as high-risk before they are created or implemented in Greece. It addresses all selected areas by embedding equality accessibility and diversity checks into its framework. It includes sections for gender children disability and cultural or linguistic impacts (Part II pages 13-14 Part III pages 20-22).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: GenAI Education Frontier - National Program for Educator Training in Generative Artificial Intelligence
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives;  AI Literacy
cse1_justif: The GenAI Education Frontier program by the Tipping Point (TTP) NGO enhances both reskilling and AI literacy by training over 1.000 educators across Greece especially in rural and island regions on the responsible and effective use of Generative AI tools in teaching. Through interactive workshops teachers gain practical digital and AI skills enabling them to integrate GenAI into curricula guide students in ethical AI use and act as local multipliers of digital transformation in their communities.
cse2_name: SciFY Academy event: “AI & Circular Economy”
cse2_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse2_contributions: AI Literacy;  Reskilling and upskilling initiatives
cse2_justif: The “AI & Circular Economy” event by SciFY Academy promotes AI literacy and upskilling among SME professionals introducing them to the practical applications of Artificial Intelligence in sustainable business models. Experts from research NGOs and industry presented how AI tools can support the Circular Economy while participants learned about free EU-funded training programs (AI4GreenSME) to develop essential digital and green skills. The event empowers SMEs to adapt to the dual transition of AI and sustainability.

cse1_name: Creation of Greek Artificial Intelligence Network (GAIN) - Network for the Protection and Promotion of Human Rights Democracy and the Rule of Law during the Development and Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems in Greece
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Consumer Protection;  Safety and Security;  Access to Redress and Remedy
cse1_justif: The GAIN Declaration promotes the protection of fundamental rights in the development and use of AI systems explicitly referencing data protection consumer protection security and access to justice. It calls for a network of 17 founding organizations to ensure compliance with the Greek Constitution the EU Charter of Fundamental Rights and the ECHR. It goes beyond legal compliance encouraging ethical responsibility and transparency in AI governance. The initiative is coordinated by Homo Digitalis with the support of the European Artificial Intelligence & Society Fund (EAISF) and is open to any interested organization.
cse2_name: Freedom of Information Request (FOIA) to the Greek Ministry of Education regarding the “OpenAI for Greece” Memorandum of Cooperation
cse2_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse2_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy
cse2_justif: The initiative addresses data protection and privacy by requesting public access to the “OpenAI for Greece” Memorandum and accompanying documents to verify compliance with GDPR Law 4624/2019 and Law 4961/2022 (p. 4-5). It raises concerns over the legal basis for processing data categories collected from teachers and students storage duration and whether a Data Protection Impact Assessment was conducted ensuring transparency and accountability in the use of AI tools in education (p. 2-3).
cse3_name: Seminar: “Artificial Intelligence and the General Data Protection Regulation” - Hellenic Centre for European Constitutional Law
cse3_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse3_justif: The seminar trains legal professionals and data protection officers on how AI systems intersect with the GDPR’s core principles such as lawfulness purpose limitation data minimization accuracy transparency and accountability.
cse4_name: Researcher’s Night: Synthetic Content and Misinformation in the Age of Artificial Intelligence
cse4_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse4_contributions: AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence
cse4_justif: It is a public event aimed at informing and raising awareness about misinformation and the use of artificial intelligence to address it. It functions primarily as an activity for knowledge dissemination and public engagement. The event was organized by the Centre for Research and Technology Hellas (CERTH) and the Media Analysis Verification and Retrieval group (MeVer) of the Information Technologies Institute (ITI). The MeVer group has extensive experience and expertise in the area of online disinformation with an emphasis on multimedia-mediated disinformation and has emerged as a result of research in several projects on social media mining information retrieval and multimodal Artificial Intelligence.
cse5_name: FRIA (Fundamental Rights Impact Assessment) Proposal by Rythmisis National Institute of Artificial Intelligence Personal Data and Digital Governance Law
cse5_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse5_contributions: Impact Assessments;  Data Protection and Privacy;  Access to Redress and Remedy
cse5_justif: The FRIA Standard provides a structured method to assess AI impacts on fundamental rights under Art. 27 of the AI Act. It operationalizes privacy through “Privacy & data governance” checks - lawfulness user control security and governance/DPO measures (p. 23). It links FRIA to GDPR DPIA requiring FRIA to complement Art. 35 DPIAs (pp. 41 43). It embeds redress by referencing effective-remedy principles and requiring procedures to inform affected persons and complaint mechanisms (p. 11).

cse1_name: “Use of AI for disinformation campaigns in Honduras”
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: The initiative raises awareness about the ethical and societal risks of AI specifically its use to manipulate information ecosystems during political campaigns. It also seeks to inform and educate the public and promote accountability around AI-driven disinformation which directly affects the representation of marginalized voices and democratic discourse. It is part of a series of alerts and articles produced by C-Libre warning about AI-generated disinformation/deepfakes circulating in Honduras.
cse2_name: Operation Misinformation
cse2_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse2_contributions: AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence
cse2_justif: The investigative report “Digital Troops of Disinformation in Honduras” addresses AI-facilitated misinformation and violence by documenting and exposing the internal operation of state-linked networks. Based on interviews with current and former public employees the report analyzes how coordinated online structures use automated accounts bots and algorithmic manipulation to spread propaganda distort public debate and attack opponents on social media. While not a technical AI governance instrument it contributes to public understanding of how digital infrastructures—potentially including automated tools—facilitate misinformation and political harassment including gendered online violence.

cse1_name: Hungary’s new biometric surveillance laws violate the AI Act
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: The Hungarian Civil Liberties Union’s legal analysis examines Hungary’s 2025 facial-recognition use. It addresses Transparency and Explainability by clarifying the operation and regulatory classification of the system. It explains in accessible terms how CCTV or protest footage is processed through automated facial comparison against a government database enabling rapid identification in infraction procedures. It also argues that the system should be treated as “real-time” remote biometric identification under the EU AI Act rather than a retrospective (“post”) system meaning stricter safeguards should apply. cse1_name: Legal analysis of Hungary’s biometric surveillance laws
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: Társaság a Szabadságjogokért (Hungarian Civil Liberties Union HCLU) blog post is providing a legal analysis of Hungarian legislation related to AI-based biometric surveillance. The analysis examines how the law enables the identification and tracking of individuals in public spaces using biometric data such as facial recognition often without their knowledge or meaningful consent. It highlights risks related to unlawful data processing insufficient safeguards and disproportionate surveillance powers. By explaining these legal and rights-based implications in accessible language the blog post raises public awareness about privacy data protection and security risks linked to AI-driven biometric monitoring.

cse1_name: The Centre for Internet and Society’s Comments and Recommendations on the Report on AI Governance Guidelines Development
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse1_justif: The Centre for Internet and Society submission is a formal civil society response to the Government of India’s Report on AI Governance Guidelines Development. It addresses Human Oversight by emphasising human-in-the-loop systems and critiquing a post-facto AI incident database (Section II; IV.3 p.6). It advances Transparency through calls for an accessible incident database and third-party audits (IV.3–4 p.6) and supports Fairness by warning against exclusion and bias from poor data quality (II.A; III p.3–4).

cse3_name: The Role of Generative AI in Shaping Human Rights and Gender Equity: A Critical Analysis
cse3_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse3_justif: This research aims to explore the dual role of generative AI technology in supporting human rights activism and gender equality. The primary issue addressed is how AI can empower individuals and organizations in advocating for human rights while also posing risks such as the spread of misinformation and the perpetuation of bias. The conclusion emphasizes the importance of creating strategies that maximize the benefits of AI while minimizing its risks. Thus this research contributes to efforts to create an AI-driven future that not only supports human rights but also promotes gender equality and it encourages further discussions about the boundaries between humans and machines in the context of these rights (Page 9-14).

cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_justif: The event highlightes the importance of inclusive skills-based approaches that align closely with civil society priorities particularly in reaching rural communities and marginalised groups often excluded from digital transformation. Through discussions on alternative education pathways such as micro-credentials bootcamps and online learning the event reinforces the role that CSOs can play in community-level skills development lifelong learning and worker transitions in response to AI-driven labour market changes. The event creates space for civil society actors to advocate for equitable workforce policies support reskilling initiatives and ensure that AI adoption contributes to inclusive and just labour outcomes in Indonesia.

cse1_name: Roundtable Discussion: Harnessing AI in the Public Service in Ireland
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_justif: The University College Dublin(UCD) Geary Institute for Public Policy and Centre for Applied Data Analytics( CeADAR) roundtable highlighted that the challenges of implementing artificial intelligence in Ireland's public services pivot on establishing the security and safety of these systems.The third concern of the roundtable- challenges facing the responsible integration of AI into public services including cultural resistance are fundamentally linked to establishing system safety and security which demands meeting rigorous regulatory requirements and resolving critical questions of public trust and accountability.
cse2_name: The Irish Council for Civil Liberties (ICCL) v Microsoft (RTB Representative Action Ireland)
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Consumer Protection;  Access to Redress and Remedy;  Data Protection and Privacy;  Data Sharing and Access
cse2_justif: The Irish Council for Civil Liberties (ICCL) led litigation by initiating a representative action against Microsoft before the Irish High Court (26 May 2025). The case challenges the use of real-time bidding (RTB) systems which rely on automated profiling and algorithmic decision-making to process personal data (e.g. IP address location browsing behaviour) and match users with targeted advertising in real time. It explicitly addresses data protection and privacy and data sharing and access as such data is allegedly broadcast to numerous third parties. Brought under Ireland’s Representative Actions Act it also supports consumer protection and constitutes access to redress and remedy through collective enforcement of digital rights.
cse3_name: IPPOSI launches Citizens’ Jury report on AI in Healthcare
cse3_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse3_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Data Sharing and Access
cse3_justif: The Citizens' Jury process was a crucial exercise in deliberative democracy that engaged citizens directly in formulating ethical policy regarding the most sensitive aspects of AI in healthcare: data protection and data sharing.Informed Deliberation on Data Ethics: The process convened 24 demographically representative citizens over 12 weeks for structured learning. They cross-examined expert witnesses with a focus on understanding the ethical issues inherent in AI's reliance on patient data.Citizen-Led Mandate for Data Governance: The final report representing a citizen-led verdict with 25 recommendationsprioritizing data security transparency and interoperable digital infrastructure.
cse4_name: Accounting for Workers in the Age of AI
cse4_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse4_contributions: Safety and Security
cse4_justif: Robustness and Cybersecurity (Page 12): The report states that systems deemed 'high risk' must comply with strict obligations before entering the market including demonstrating high levels of robustness cybersecurity and accuracy.

cse1_name: People Manage People Via Machines
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Human Oversight and Determination;  Transparency and Explainability;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse1_justif: This is a research project held within the Israel Democracy Institute aimed at addressing the different challenges of using AI to govern and administrate. It includes inter alia research into the Public use of AI Transparency publications and public consultation. The project also includes workshops with the participation of government officials. See for example participation of the Head of the Privacy Authority https://shorturl.at/IY4bT (8/9/25) and post of the Head of Israel Digital Agency after participating in IDI Dec 1st event: https://www.linkedin.com/posts/shiralevami_%D7%94%D7%A9%D7%91%D7%95%D7%A2-%D7%94%D7%A9%D7%AA%D7%AA%D7%A4%D7%AA%D7%99-%D7%9B%D7%93%D7%95%D7%91%D7%A8%D7%AA-%D7%91%D7%9B%D7%A0%D7%A1-%D7%94%D7%97%D7%96%D7%99%D7
cse2_name: HCJ 4271/24 Ass'n for Civ. Rts. in Isr. v. Isr. Police (Isr. Sup. Ct. 2024)
cse2_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse2_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability
cse2_justif: Fairness and non-discrimination are advanced through the critique of AI-based policing decisions that rely on opaque statistical correlations and biased datasets emphasizing the risk of profiling and unequal treatment and insisting that enforcement decisions must avoid discriminatory considerations and be based on rational individualized grounds (Sections C; E; Legal Argument – Violation of Equality). Transparency is promoted by highlighting the “black box” problem and arguing that decisions affecting liberty must be explainable reviewable and justified to both the individual and the courts as a condition for due process and accountability (Sections B; Legal Argument – Explainability and Accountability).
cse3_name: People Manage People Via Machines - Research Project and Event Series
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_contributions: Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability
cse3_justif: This project conducted a comprehensive survey 'People Manage People via Machines' investigating AI’s impact on human capital management. Findings were presented at the Israel Bar Association the Israeli Industrial Relations Research Association 2025 and the Privacy Protection Authority 2025 to raise awareness.The survey specifically addresses:Fairness: Identifies significant concerns regarding built-in bias in automated recruitment.Transparency: Highlights a lack of written policies for autonomous decision-making and insufficient employee training.Human Oversight: The report provides clear evidence of a 'unanimous agreement' across sectors that humans must retain the final word in AI-driven decisions to mitigate ethical risks.
cse4_name: People Manage People Via Machines Research Project Survey Results
cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse4_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse4_justif: airness and Non-DiscriminationPage 31: 11% of lawyers reported encountering issues of 'employee or candidate discrimination' when advising clients.Page 32: 23% of lawyers identified 'fear of improper discrimination' as a legal challenge during the implementation of these technologies.Page 35: 30% of lawyers believe government regulation is specifically required for 'protection against discrimination'.Transparency and ExplainabilityPage 19: Results show that most organizations (54%-71%) lack a written policy regarding 'decisions technologies can make independently' (explainability).Page 21: Covers transparency regarding data rights showing only 31-48% of organizations allow employees to view or correct collected data.

cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives;  Labour Protections;  AI Literacy
cse1_justif: The initiative protects workers by addressing discrimination surveillance privacy and weakened unions in Part A: Micro/Meso/Macro Risks and by recommending notification duties and privacy/anti-discrimination rules in Israel – Level Three. It supports reskilling through STEM and workforce training in South Korea U.S. and Israel – Level Four. AI literacy is promoted via guidance tools and training in Israel – Level Two and broader digital-literacy strategies in Nordic Countries Germany and South Korea.
