Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Comments on the Draft Regulations of Law 31814 a law that promotes the use of AI for the country's economic and social development - Peru
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Impact Assessments
cse1_justif: In 2024 Hiperderecho a Peruvian nonprofit CSO sent a letter with comments on the law when it was still a draft open to public consultation. Point 10 “Need to Incorporate Fundamental Rights Impact Studies” mentions the need for the law to include the obligation to carry out human rights impact assessments prior to the development and implementation of AI-based systems. As part of these assessments it highlights the need to evaluate the impact on privacy and personal data protection. Also Point 8.1 they refer to privacy as a matter of importance when it comes to the deployment of biometric technologies powered by AI advocating for its inclusion on the 'forbidden uses' category.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: Code of Ethics for AI Professionals - An Executive Summary
cse1_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse1_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination;  Environmental Impact
cse1_justif: The code promotes: Transparency and Explainability Fairness and Equity... Human-centricity ...and Environmental Sustainability. It recommends that AI professionals: 1. Contribute to the betterment of society and to human and environmental well-being; 3. Be honest trustworthy and act with integrity. AI professionals should be transparent about system capabilities limitations and potential problems...; 4. Strive for fairness and guard against discrimination.. AI professionals are strongly encouraged to develop and deploy systems in ways that promote fairness and respect for all...; 5. AI professionals... should contribute to assessments that support responsible design deployment and oversight... (pp. 1-2)

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: Philippine Skills Framework for Analytics & AI (PSF-AAI)
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives;  AI Literacy
cse1_justif: The Philippine Skills Framework for Analytics & AI (PSF-AAI) is an endeavor developed for the Philippine workforce of all industries to address skills mastery and continuous learning in the AAI Sector. It is a collaboration spearheaded by the Department of Information and Communication Technology (DICT) and the Analytics and Artificial Intelligence Association of the Philippines (AAP). The seven tracks of the PSF-AAI consist of (1) Business Intelligence & Strategy (2) Data Stewardship (3) Data Engineering (4) Data Science (5) AI Engineering (6) Applied Data/AI Research and (7) Analytics/AI Education. The AAP is a non-profit organization composed of various stakeholders using Analytics and AI for the good of society.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Code of Ethics for AI Professionals - An Executive Summary
cse1_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse1_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Safety and Security
cse1_justif: The Analytics and Artificial Intelligence Association of the Philippines (AAP) a non-profit multi-stakeholder organization published a Code of Ethics for AI Professionals that recommends promoting 'safe practices and prevent harm' 'consider the potential impacts of their decisions and mitigate harm.' (p. 2) “AI professionals should understand the rights and responsibilities associated with the collection and use of personal information taking precautions to prevent re-identification of anonymized data.” (p. 2) 'AI professionals must 'design and implement AI systems that are robustly and securely engineered ensuring privacy protection and security by design.' (p. 3)

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: Panoptykon Foundation
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: The Panoptykon Foundation actively participates in the legislative process by submitting official opinions on the draft act on artificial intelligence systems currently being worked on by the Ministry of Digitalization. They propose that the legislature specify the concrete tasks covered by the exemption rather than applying a general national security clause. Panoptykon intervenes regarding the design of the procedure for filing complaints against AI systems. In its opinion it requests the removal of the requirement for the complainant to provide the name of the AI system arguing that for an ordinary citizen obtaining this name might be impossible (e.g. due to trade secrets) which would block the ability to assert their rights.
cse2_name: Complaint against Twitter for using personal data for unspecified forms of machine learning or artificial intelligence
cse2_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse2_contributions: Transparency and Explainability
cse2_justif: Panoptykon took action after discovering that X had quietly enabled a setting to use the personal data of its users (posts interactions) to train its Grok AI model. It was filing a formal legal complaint to the Polish Data Protection Authority (UODO). Shortly after the coordinated complaints were filed X agreed to suspend the processing of EU users' data for Grok training. addresses transparency by challenging the unlawful use of EU users’ data to train AI without explicit consent. In the “Summary” (pp. 3–4) and Sections 3.1–3.2 (pp. 4–6) it details the lack of notification default opt-in settings and vague AI purposes arguing violations of GDPR lawfulness and transparency principles.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: AI on the polish labour market
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives;  Labour Protections
cse1_justif: The report highlights that women are more exposed to AI disruptions (28% of working women vs. 17% of men). It warns that AI could exacerbate income inequality necessitating updated labor policies. The report notes that Poland ranks sixth from the bottom in the EU for adult educational efforts. It advocates for Swedish-style 'training leave' and 'Individual Training Accounts' to facilitate reskilling.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Poland regulates AI systems. What needs to be improved in the draft law?
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Safety and Security;  Access to Redress and Remedy
cse1_justif: The Panoptykon Foundation submitted a position paper (27 Feb 2025 see drive link) on Poland’s draft Act on Artificial Intelligence Systems. The document raises concerns about exemptions allowing security services to use AI without sufficient oversight (Section 1: “Exemptions for AI in the area of internal security and constitutional order”). It also proposes improvements to the complaint mechanism for individuals affected by AI decisions including removing excessive requirements such as providing the system’s name (Section 2: “Complaint about a violation of the provisions of the Regulation or Act”).
cse2_name: Internal Rules for the Use of Artificial Intelligence. A Guide for Social Organizations
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Safety and Security
cse2_justif: The Sektor 3.0 published the guide “Internal Rules for the Use of Artificial Intelligence. A Guide for Social Organizations” (29 Sept 2025). The publication provides recommendations for NGOs on the safe and ethical use of AI tools. It addresses risks related to data protection legal compliance and responsible AI deployment. The guide includes practical guidance on developing internal AI policies protecting and anonymizing confidential data and managing AI-related risks (Sections on law security ethics and values and data management). The initiative aims to strengthen safe and responsible AI use in civil society organizations.
cse3_name: AI Task Force
cse3_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse3_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Safety and Security;  Impact Assessments
cse3_justif: The AI Task Force launched in November 2024 and coordinated by CSO - Fundacja Centrum Cyfrowe and partner organizations provides interdisciplinary support for organizations developing AI systems. The initiative offers legal ethical and technical guidance across the AI lifecycle including risk analysis compliance with data protection regulations and cybersecurity considerations. It also conducts reviews and audits of AI systems to evaluate their effectiveness risks and regulatory compliance. Through advisory support monitoring and evaluation of AI projects the initiative promotes safer and more accountable development and deployment of AI technologies.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: Ai.ethics Program
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse1_justif: The AI.Ethics Program enhances Human Oversight and Determination by auditing AI systems through structured self-assessment that identifies the human responsible for ethical compliance and ensures accountability in decision-making. The audit promotes Transparency and Explainability by evaluating algorithms data quality and governance maturity based on ISO and OECD frameworks producing reports that clearly communicate results and improvement actions. It addresses ethical human rights and social impacts by assessing fairness privacy and bias prevention (check it here: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1Ffe9mJsMp-PWorKCB91d3UXh5Ma3zPJV/view?usp=drive_link).
cse2_name: Bridge AI
cse2_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse2_contributions: Transparency and Explainability
cse2_justif: The Bridge AI project brings together diverse actors from society including industry leaders professors and researchers to discuss responsible AI governance through collaborative workshops and to produce a technical report for public use.One of the panels addresses the question: What should be included in an AI risk assessment tool for public and private entities?Preparatory work included benchmarking existing risk assessment tools and identifying key gaps in the Guide for AI: Ethical Risk Assessment Tool. The workshop resulted it promoting greater clarity on how AI systems operate including their purpose and data sources (https://drive.google.com/file/d/1FbiP0kcBmmIcNoxDaTdbNccMQdL2Ak6u/view?usp=drive_link)
cse3_name: Right to Information: For a Reliable and Trustworthy AI
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse3_justif: The DECO guideline “Right to Information: For a Reliable and Trustworthy AI” promotes Transparency and Explainability by requiring clear information on AI decisions traceability of data sources and algorithmic transparency. It supports Human Oversight and Determination through recommendations for continuous human verification error correction and accountability mechanisms. For Fairness and Non Discrimination it emphasizes equitable treatment accessibility and prevention of discriminatory outcomes in AI systems.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: DeepSeek: DECO PROteste files complaint in defense of Portuguese citizens' personal data
cse1_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse1_justif: The initiative by DECO PROteste consists of a formal complaint to the Comissão Nacional de Proteção de Dados (CNPD) regarding the data-processing practices of DeepSeek a generative-AI service. The article explicitly flags the absence of age-verification mechanisms and the risk of processing data of users under 18 years old which implicates children’s rights.
cse2_name: AI applied to education and pedagogical practices
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Children’s Rights
cse2_justif: The 21st edition of Safer Internet Day was organized by the Safe Internet Center Consortium a multi-stakeholder initiative involving public authorities NGOs and other partners including the Portuguese Association for Victim Support Altice Foundation and Microsoft Portugal. In addition the event brought together teachers other professionals and young people to discuss myths advantages disadvantages and the use of AI in education as well as issues related to young people.
cse3_name: Two Fingers of Conversation - 23rd session: Artificial Intelligence in Visual Disability
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_justif: The initiative by the Association of the Blind and Partially Sighted of Portugal (ACAPO) — the 23rd session of the talk series “Two Fingers of Conversation” titled “Artificial Intelligence in Visual Disability” — focuses on the intersection of AI and the rights of persons with visual impairments.
cse4_name: National Report on Accessibility to Justice by APAV & FENACERCI
cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse4_contributions: Children’s Rights;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities
cse4_justif: The National Report by APAV & FENACERCI aims “to collect information on the topic of access to justice for child victims with disabilities” (p. 11 of the PDF). It also seeks to “examine the provisions of the United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities (UNCRPD)” (p. 16 of the PDF). Chapter 4 contains a specific section focusing on how technology and AI are used within the criminal justice system to improve victims’ access to justice (pp. 32–36 of the PDF). The report highlights the importance of “upgrading the technological infrastructure of courtrooms to enable streamlined processes and facilitate the judge's interventions and decisions ensuring equitable access to justice for all.'(p. p. 35 of the PDF).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: IMPULSO IA: APDC + Google national AI literacy programme for professionals
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: IMPULSO IA an APDC initiative plus Google aims to equip professionals across sectors with practical AI skills to enhance productivity and support digital transformation. The program is free accessible and requires no prior AI knowledge. Registration opened on 30 October 2024 and remains ongoing until March 2026. It offers four updated modules: fundamentals and responsible AI; practical AI for business and SMEs; AI for daily workplace tasks; and AI for marketing. Through hands-on demonstrations using tools such as Gemini Workspace NotebookLM and GA4 the program enables participants to apply AI strategically in real work settings. As a national skill-building initiative IMPULSO IA directly contributes to the Upskilling Initiatives.
cse2_name: Bridge AI Conference: Portugal at the Forefront of Artificial Intelligence Regulation
cse2_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse2_contributions: AI Literacy
cse2_justif: Bridge AI founded by young science managers and led by Professor Helena Moniz aims to place Portugal at the forefront of AI Act implementation by fostering a national ecosystem for responsible innovation. The Bridge AI Conference brings together policymakers experts and civil-society actors to debate AI risks ethics and strategies to increase AI literacy (p.2). The booklet shows strong CSO engagement in the Labour & Skills dimension e.g. Vasco Pedro contributed to the session on “Artificial Intelligence: Literacy and Advanced Training” and Joe Paton presented the working group’s results on advanced training and AI literacy (p.7) demonstrating active CSO participation in shaping national AI governance.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: DECO Guidelines on Reliable and Trustworthy Artificial Intelligence
cse1_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse1_justif: DECO’s guideline evaluates four AI assistants and identifies errors omissions and lack of sourcing that undermine the reliability of consumer-facing information (pp. 3–6; 10–17 of the PDF). The average response quality is 2/5 revealing significant risks of misinformation about legal rights including warranties withdrawal periods and essential public services (pp. 18–19 of the PDF). The guidelines call for transparency traceability human oversight continuous monitoring and AI literacy to protect consumers (pp. 20–27 of the PDF). The goal of this guideline is to empower consumers to use AI in a safe and trustworthy way (p. 6 of the PDF).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: Hamad Bin Khalifa University and Qatar Charity organize workshop on empowering charities and non-profits through artificial intelligence
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_contributions: Human Oversight and Determination;  Transparency and Explainability
cse1_justif: Hamad Bin Khalifa University (HBKU) and Qatar Charity (QC) collaborated and organized a three-day workshop titled “Empowering Charities and Non-Profits through Artificial Intelligence” to explore the transformative potential of AI in the humanitarian and development sectors. In the opening it was noted that AI offers tools that can enhance the way we deliver aid track impact and make faster more informed decisions—tools that must be applied thoughtfully and responsibly thus directly addressing Human Oversight and Determination. Day two sessions explicitly explored and focused on  governance and transparency.
cse2_name: WISE 11 Summit Report & Policy Recommendations Creative Fluency: Human Flourishing in the Age of Artificial Intelligence.
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Human Oversight and Determination
cse2_justif: The WISE 11 Summit Highlights report produced by WISE (World Innovation Summit for Education) and an initiative of the Qatar Foundation which organizes the summit and publishes its research outputs addresses in the section on Classrooms of the Future that artificial intelligence systems should support not replace human decision-making and highlights the importance of teacher agency pedagogical judgment and human-led governance in AI-enabled learning environments. The recommendations underline that accountability for educational outcomes remains with human actors ensuring meaningful human control over the design and use of artificial intelligence in educational systems.

cse1_name: National Reporting Service for Child Sexual Abuse Material SigurOnline Hotline 2024 Activity Report
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: In Moldova the International Center “La Strada” operates the Helpline and Hotline for Children and Women including the Child Helpline 116 111 and the Hotline for reporting online child sexual abuse material. The SigurOnline Hotline addresses children’s rights in relation to AI by identifying classifying and reporting AI-generated child sexual abuse material protecting children from re-victimization and harmful digital content (p.13 22–23). This service supports policy implementation by performing operational functions that are integral to the implementation of child online protection policy by receiving  reports of child sexual abuse material referring them to law enforcement authorities and relevant stakeholders.
cse2_name: Massive online networks that manipulate voting
cse2_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse2_contributions: Gender Equality;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse2_justif: Promo-LEX published research documenting the spread of AI-mediated disinformation that exploits themes of gender equality and cultural and linguistic diversity. The report shows how manipulated content and online narratives use gender stereotypes and cultural symbols to mislead audiences influence public opinion and polarize communities. By analyzing coordinated online activity Promo-LEX highlights the growing use of AI tools in producing and amplifying disinformation in Moldova’s information space.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: UNICEF-led training: 'Strengthening innovative student-centered teaching-learning practices in schools in key regions: Ungheni and Cahul through the use of digital technologies and STEAM projects'
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_contributions: AI Literacy;  Reskilling and upskilling initiatives
cse1_justif: The Online Generative AI Course for Educators in Moldova implemented by UNICEF and funded by the European Union under the EU4Moldova: Focal Regions programme supports the upskilling of teachers by enhancing digital and AI literacy. Piloted in 12 schools from Cahul and Ungheni it involved around 400 students and 100 teachers who developed STEAM projects using generative AI tools. Developed with Moldovan Association of ICT Companies (ATIC) and the Ministry of Education and Research the course available online promotes responsible AI integration in education.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: CSO led media Literacy workshop at Moldova's State University: Young journalists concerned about fake news and disinformation through artificial intelligence
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_contributions: Safety and Security;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence
cse1_justif: A workshop organised by a Moldovan CSO Center for Independent Journalism directly addressed safety and security by training journalism students to critically assess online information and recognize manipulative or harmful digital content. Through interactive exercises on fake news emotional manipulation and deepfakes it strengthened participants’ ability to verify sources and ensure information integrity. It also tackled AI-facilitated misinformation and disinformation by analyzing examples of AI-generated deepfake videos and discussing how artificial intelligence can be used to distort facts and create deceptive narratives.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: The Generative Generation: Young People and Artificial Intelligence in the Social Media Era
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: The “Generative Generation” project was organised by the Association for Technology and Internet (ApTI) in partnership with two other Romanian civil society organisations and comprised nine workshops held at universities and other youth-focused events. The workshops covered topics such as social media algorithms chatbots digital footprints and legislation governing artificial intelligence. The materials used and shared explored the legal and institutional ecosystem of privacy algorithms in social media democracy and political content and digital rights. The session on social media algorithms examined the lack of transparency in the AI models used by platforms to promote specific types of content.
cse2_name: World Cafe Event by CSO: “Eco-AI: How Artificial Intelligence Is Redefining the Rules of the Clean Industry?”
cse2_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse2_contributions: Environmental Impact
cse2_justif: The “Eco-AI: How Artificial Intelligence Is Redefining the Rules of the Clean Industry?” world cafe event held by the CSO EUROPE DIRECT Bucharest on 5 June 2025 addressed the environmental impact of AI by exploring how AI technologies interact with clean industry and sustainability goals. Through four thematic discussion tables covering AI factories in Romania labour market implications ecological standards and digital transformation and AI regulations for sustainability participants discussed integrating AI without compromising environmental objectives. The event highlighted the need to adapt public policies research and education ecosystems to ensure AI development supports EU environmental targets and a responsible green transit

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: “AI for All” Programme – Artificial Intelligence for People with Disabilities
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: The “AI for All” programme organised by the CSO AbilityHub Romania with the involvement of RoboHub.ai aims to promote the digital rights and inclusion of people with disabilities through training in the use and development of AI tools. It offers specialised AI courses and digital skills training tailored to disabled individuals focusing on accessible personalised technologies and empowering participants to use AI for independent living and employment. By providing accessible learning resources and direct support the CSO enables people with disabilities to participate fully in the digital society reducing exclusion and ensuring equal access to AI opportunities. This is the programme’s second edition following the first in early 2024.
cse2_name: WiDS Romania 5th Edition: The Evolution of Artificial Intelligence. From Hype to Trust and Safety
cse2_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse2_contributions: Gender Equality
cse2_justif: The WiDS Romania Conference “The Evolution of Artificial Intelligence: From Hype to Trust and Safety” provides a platform dedicated to women in data science and AI. Organised by Think Tank 360 and Women in Data Science Romania it brings together female experts researchers and innovators to showcase their work through panels and mentoring. WiDS promotes women’s visibility leadership and participation in technology supports skills development and advocates inclusive innovation. The conference featured speed mentoring and panels on AI safety ethical responsibilities and academia’s role in AI development.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: Robohub.ai WISE Project
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: In May 2025 the Romanian CSO Robohub.ai launched the WISE Project designed to train 230 young women and girls in data science and artificial intelligence. The programme which was announced on 15 May combines technical expertise with the development of non-technical skills. The aim is to reduce the gender gap in digital education and to reskill young women in AI. It also offers participants mentoring and support services including childcare during in-person training sessions.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: CSO Coalition Open Letter Against Deepfake Bill
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: A coalition of 12 Romanian civil society organisations led by ActiveWatch—a CSO advocating for free communication in the public interest—published an open letter opposing a deepfake bill nearing approval in Parliament. They criticised the version presented to MPs describing it as a “Chinese version” of the original draft discussed during public consultations. The organisations objected to the introduction of prison sentences for creating or distributing deepfake content without exceptions for artistic use as provided in the EU AI Act. They also warned that the bill could legitimise malicious deepfakes provided they are correctly labelled as AI-generated.
cse2_name: Digital Skills Academy Program
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence;  Safety and Security
cse2_justif: Between January and June 2025 Accenture Romania together with two civil society partners – Teach for Romania and the Roma Education Fund – delivered the fifth edition of the Digital Skills Academy Programme in 102 schools from vulnerable communities across the country. The Digital Skills Programme offered digital skills courses including modules focused on online safety deepfakes and artificial intelligence to 2300 pupils aged between 8 and 14. The children were taught how to recognise disinformation and fraud schemes that make use of deepfakes and AI technology as well as how to use AI responsibly.

cse1_name: BIRN's Investigative Reporting on AI Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: Balkan Investigative Reporting Network BIRN regional NGO media reported on AI systems’ use in Serbia highlighting opaque algorithmic decision-making potential biases and accountability gaps. Through investigative journalism it informed the public and policymakers about AI deployment ethical concerns and systemic risks supporting calls for clearer more explainable AI practices. Stories included examples of AI-driven profiling or automated decisions showing real-world impacts and the need for transparency in algorithmic systems. The initiative is part of BIRN's broader reporting about AI and its effects on the rights of Serbian citizens.

cse1_name: Future of Work: AI Platform Labour & Algorithmic Management
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: The AMPLIFY project and The Future of work (FOW) project by Re:People.rs Centre for Society and Technology (CSO) advances labour protections by analysing how AI-enabled platform work and algorithmic management affect workers in Serbia and advocating for full implementation of the EU Platform Work Directive. It convenes stakeholders publishes evidence and highlights gaps in rights social protection and regulation for platform and gig workers affected by automated systems. Through research and public events it informs policy debates on safeguarding worker rights in AI-mediated labour.

cse1_name: Digital Rights Review: AI-Driven Violations Go Unchecked in South-East Europe
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Safety and Security;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence
cse1_justif: The initiative is led by the Balkan Investigative Reporting Network (BIRN) (CSO) through its Digital Rights Review which documents and analyzes AI-related digital rights violations in South-East Europe. It primarily addresses Safety and Security and AI-facilitated misinformation and violence by investigating how AI technologies are used in harmful ways including protest surveillance election manipulation deepfakes and digital sexual abuse. Through investigative reporting and analysis the initiative raises public awareness about these risks and highlights gaps in oversight and accountability related to AI use in the region.

cse1_name: The Digitruck Salone Project
cse1_drive: No applicable document has been publicly published
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: The Digitruck Salone Project contributes to Gender Equality and Children’s Rights by expanding access to digital and AI-enabled learning for women adolescent girls and youth in rural communities where formal education and technology access are limited. By intentionally targeting girls and women the project addresses gender disparities in digital inclusion and future economic participation. The initiative integrates introductory AI training and AI-enabled learning tools including the use of AfriGPT on 4G-enabled smartphones to support digital skills development and problem-solving. Since its launch in May 2024 over 300 girls and women entrepreneurs have been reached advancing children’s right to inclusive quality and future-orient.
cse2_name: TheTeacher.AI – AI-Assisted Professional Development for Teachers in Sierra Leone
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Children’s Rights;  Gender Equality
cse2_justif: TheTeacher.AI developed by EducAid Sierra Leone and Fab Data supported 193 teachers across 122 schools using an AI-powered WhatsApp chatbot for lesson planning and professional development. Implemented Jan–Jul 2024 it enhanced teaching quality and access to learning resources directly advancing children’s right to quality education. Balanced teacher participation promoted gender equality and low-bandwidth. A report published in Feb 2024 documented early field observations detailing teacher engagement usage patterns and outcomes (Section 2: Context pp.1–2; Section 3.4: Tailoring for Local Conditions pp.3–4; Section 4 pp.4–5; Section 5: How Teachers Use AI pp.5–6).

cse1_justif: EducAid’s initiative contributes to reskilling/upskilling and AI literacy by providing structured training and ongoing coaching for teachers on how to use AI tools in lesson planning assessment and classroom delivery. Teachers learn to generate tailored learning activities verify AI outputs and integrate AI into low-resource environments. This continuous support builds practical skills strengthens digital competency and enhances educators’ confidence in using AI for teaching.

cse1_name: Singapore AI Safety Hub (SASH)
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: SASH primarily addresses Safety and Security through technical research on AI risks and control mechanisms. Members conduct research on AI alignment interpretability adversarial robustness and AI deception detection. The Hub hosted a Technical Workshop on Misalignment & Control examining evidence of AI deception and strategic behavior (July 2025) and organized the AI Safety Research Exchange featuring presentations on mechanistic interpretability refusal interpretation and agent security.? SASH contributes to Impact Assessments through involvement in developing the Singapore Consensus on Global AI Safety Research Priorities (June 2025) embers participate in capability evaluations and model safety testing.

cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: Research project of a non-profit research institute - KiNiT and is a series of blogs and scientific engagement with the goal to “fundamentally change the oversight of social media AI algorithms like recommender systems or search engines and their tendencies to spread and promote harmful content. The main ground-breaking idea of this proposal is a novel paradigm of model-based algorithmic auditing.” Further information about workshop and conference engagements on this topic can be found in blogs under this linked article /on the website.

cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: The SensAI research project by a Slovak research institute KInIT is dedicated to bridging the gap between AI technology human rights and ethics with a special focus on low-resource languages. SensAI enhances large language models (LLMs) for Slovak making AI tools accessible for speakers of underrepresented languages while evaluating their performance against multilingual AI approaches.

cse1_name: KInIT and the Slovak Academy of Sciences advocate for cleared guidelines on red lines in the EU AI Act
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: Research institute KInIT and the Slovak Academy of Sciences submitted contributions to consultation on the European Union’s AI Act focusing on prohibited practices outlined in Article 5. For example they highlighted concerns about widely used recommender systems particularly on social media that already engage in problematic behaviours affecting vulnerable individuals. Its input also addressed social scoring which is not explicitly defined in the AI Act but aligns with behavioural analysis conducted by social media platforms. Concerns were raised about unjustified or disproportionate treatment of individuals through content personalisation and the formation of disinformation-driven filter bubbles.
cse2_name: InfoGuard Academy
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence
cse2_justif: Educational programme InfoGuard Academy was training young experts in the field of combating disinformation and other types of information manipulation. The program was overseen by the security and analytical think tank Adapt Institute and the online platform Infosecurity.sk dedicated to monitoring the information environment and topics related to information and cognitive security.
cse3_name: AI Awards 2025: Trustworthy AI
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_justif: The theme of the AI Awards 2025 was Trustworthy AI and entries were judged according several criteria including Societal and environmental impacts; reliability and safety; or Justice and fair play . The use of artificial intelligence opens the door to key ethical questions such as the impact of AI systems on human autonomy their reliability security and privacy protection. The award is organized by Hopero the European Center for Digital Innovations in Slovakia and the Kempelen Institute of Intelligent Technologies (KInIT) an independent AI research institute in Slovakia

cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.

cse4_justif: 'This article contributes to a better understanding of the ethical and fundamental rights risks of using wearable sleep monitoring devices and thus helps to improve the safety of their use.' The research is based in a Slovak research institute on AI KiniT.

cse5_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse5_justif: In this report–based on the information shared and lessons learned– Slovak think thank GLOBSEC and its partners present several recommendations to fight misinformation including with regards to using AI to tackle it. Link to URL I was unable to save is attached below https://web.archive.org/save/https://www.globsec.org/sites/default/files/2024-01/Enhancing%20the%20Efficiency%20of%20Combating%20Disinformation%20-%20A%20CEE%20Perspective.pdf Moreover in 2024 GLOBSEC also published a report on Regulating Deepfakes and Combatting AI-driven Manipulation https://www.globsec.org/sites/default/files/2024-12/Regulating%20Deepfakes%20-%20Global%20Approaches%20to%20Combatting%20AI-Driven%20Manipulation%20policy%20paper%20ver4%20web.pdf

cse1_name: Slovenia 5.0 – Time for a New Development Model
cse1_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Environmental Impact
cse1_justif: The Industry 5.0 Institute (CSO) has prepared a strategic document titled “A New Development Model Slovenia 5.0: From Industry 5.0 to Society 5.0”. The purpose of this initiative is to spark a broad debate about Slovenia’s future and to encourage cooperation among key stakeholders in shaping a new development paradigm. The initiative promotes a human-centric fair ethical and sustainable approach to AI within a broader Industry 5.0 development model including transparency citizen participation and environmental sustainability (pp. 2 8 10) where technology does not replacebut complements human potential (p. 2).
cse2_name: Policy Recommendations by the CSO Danes nov dan: 'How to fill the gaps in the AI Act' & Advocacy for Migrant Rights in AI
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability
cse2_justif: The initiative by Danes je nov dan addresses fairness and non-discrimination by highlighting risks of profiling and unequal impacts in migration and law enforcement contexts (section: “Conditional prohibitions for some high-risk systems”). It strongly promotes transparency and explainability criticising weak transparency rules and calling for a public AI registry and clearer oversight (section: “Insufficient transparency” and final recommendations). Human oversight is addressed indirectly through calls for judicial authorisation and independent monitoring (sections on national security and approval procedures).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: Safe.si Survey on Youth Use of Generative AI (2025)
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: Safe.si is Slovenia’s national awareness centre for the safe use of the internet operating within the Center for Safer Internet Slovenia. The project is implemented by the University of Ljubljana Faculty of Social Sciences Zavod ARNES and the Union of Friends of Youth Slovenia (Zveza prijateljev mladine Slovenije) which are non-governmental non-profit organisations (zavodi). Although one partner is a university the initiative functions as a public-interest non-academic programme focused on child and youth online safety. The 2025 survey documents widespread use of generative AI among minors low awareness of age and privacy protections and AI-enabled harms such as identity misuse supporting children’s rights and similar.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: Association of Free Trade Unions of Slovenia (ZSSS - Zveza svobodnih sindikatov Slovenije) Thematic Magazine 'Labour and Digital Transformation' (Delavstvo in digitalna preobrazba)
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: The article published by the Association of Free Trade Unions of Slovenia (ZSSS) in the September 2025 issue explains how AI and algorithmic management affect workers highlighting risks such as opaque decision-making precarity and reduced bargaining power (sections on “algorithmic management” and platform work). It discusses EU measures like the AI Act (2024) and Platform Work Directive including human oversight transparency and the right to contest automated decisions (sections “New European legislative framework” and “AI Act”).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Conference by the Danes je nov dan CSO - 'State and AI: who sets the rules of the future?'
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_contributions: Safety and Security;  Data Protection and Privacy;  Impact Assessments
cse1_justif: In September 2025 the CSO Danes je nov dan hosted a major public event and international roundtable to challenge the lack of transparency in government AI deployment. The initiative addressed 'Trust and Safety' by focusing on two high-stakes areas: algorithmic accountability in public administration and the risks of biometric surveillance by the police. By bringing together international experts and the Slovenian public the CSO advocated for a Public AI Registry to ensure that state algorithms are subject to scrutiny. The event highlighted real-world harms such as wrongful arrests due to facial recognition errors directly educating citizens on their rights to Safety Privacy and Access to Remedy.

cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.

cse2_justif: The initiative is led by Safe.si an awareness and research programme operating within the Faculty of Social Sciences at the University of Ljubljana thus qualifying as a CSO–academic actor. It directly addresses data protection and privacy by highlighting concerns around the handling of personal data in AI tools and safety and security by documenting concrete harms such as AI-generated fake images identity misuse and online violence affecting minors. It also directly engages with AI-facilitated misinformation and violence by identifying patterns of misuse including the creation of deceptive content and harmful applications of generative AI.

cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_justif: The activity led by the CSO Collaboration on International ICT Policy for East and Southern Africa is a training workshop on AI regulation trends in Africa (23–24 April 2025).  It explicitly addresses Transparency and Explainability by emphasizing that AI systems should enhance transparency and accountability and be designed in ways that are understandable and relevant to users.
cse2_name: Africa Just AI Conference
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability
cse2_justif: Research ICT Africa (based in Cape Town) organized the 2024 Africa Just AI conference. Fairness and non-discrimination was especially discussed in the context of gender with recommendations to ensure that data used to train AI is diverse and representative and that bias auditing be introduced to mitigate gender bias (pg. 34). Fairness and bias also came up in the context of data governance and justice (pg. 72 and 88). The proceedings was also recommended that 'AI developers and companies should promote transparency in AI algorithms and decision-making processes to facilitate accountability and public scrutiny' (pg. 34) with sessions focusing on transparency in AI enabled medical devices (pg. 60) and public AI transparency (pg. 130-)
cse3_name: State of Internet Freedom in Africa 2025 - Navigating the Implications of AI on Digital Democracy in Africa Report by Collaboration on International ICT Policy for East and Southern Africa (CIPESA)
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse3_justif: The activity led by the CSO Collaboration on International ICT Policy for East and Southern Africa is a research report on internet freedom and AI in Africa. It explicitly addresses Fairness and Non-Discrimination by requiring AI systems to prevent bias expand equitable access and reflect diverse populations (p. 59). It addresses Transparency and Explainability by calling for clear explanations of AI decisions and publicly accessible documentation and by recommending transparent and auditable public sector AI use (pp. 6 59). It also addresses Human Oversight and Determination by emphasizing meaningful human control and proposing institutions with mandates to oversee audit and investigate AI systems (pp. 6 59).

cse1_contributions: Gender Equality;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse1_justif: The activity led by the CSO Collaboration on International ICT Policy for East and Southern Africa (CIPESA) is a training workshop on AI regulation trends in Africa (23–24 April 2025). It explicitly addresses Gender Equality by examining AI-facilitated gender-based violence and its societal impacts including how AI systems can reinforce harm. It also addresses Cultural and Linguistic Diversity by emphasizing that AI systems should be designed around African users’ contexts and realities promoting locally relevant inclusive and context-sensitive technologies aligned with regional needs.
cse2_name: 2nd International Conference on Children's Rights - The Global Digital Future we Seek: Artificial Intelligence Online Safety and Children's Rights in the Digital Environment
cse2_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse2_contributions: Children’s Rights;  Gender Equality;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities
cse2_justif: The Nelson Mandela's Children Fund with Stellenbosch University hosted the 2nd International Conference on Children’s Rights with the theme “Artificial Intelligence Online Safety and Children’s Rights in the Digital Environment.” Over the three days of the conference the following topics were covered: AI Data Protection and Child Rights; Commercialization and Labor in Childhood; Child Participation and Agency; Equity Access and Inclusion; Gender Disability and Intersectional Risks; Systemic Weaknesses and Capacity Building; Legal Responses Regulation and Governance; School Safety and Digital Education; The Role of Non-Governmental Actors and Institutions. https://cyberbullying.org/ai-online-safety-childrens-rights-conference
cse3_name: State of Internet Freedom in Africa 2025 - Navigating the Implications of AI on Digital Democracy in Africa Report by Collaboration on International ICT Policy for East and Southern Africa (CIPESA)
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_contributions: Gender Equality;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities
cse3_justif: The activity led by the CSO Collaboration on International ICT Policy for East and Southern Africa is a research report on internet freedom and AI in Africa. It explicitly addresses Gender Equality by showing how AI systems and digital policies can reinforce structural inequalities and limit women’s access and participation online (p. 44). It addresses Cultural and Linguistic Diversity by calling for inclusive contextually relevant AI tools and warning that non-representative datasets can exclude users based on language and demographics (pp. 6 44). It also addresses Rights of Persons with Disabilities by requiring fairness and non-discrimination in AI explicitly including disability in design and governance (p. 59).
cse4_name: Gender Rights in Tech (GRIT)
cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse4_contributions: Gender Equality
cse4_justif: Cape Town-based feminist non-profit Gender Rights in Tech (GRIT) developed a trauma-informed AI chatbot providing anonymous guidance in diverse South African languages on Gender Based Violence (GBV) survivors' rights legal services and sexual reproductive health. App includes panic button evidence vault and a resource center. Has 12000 users. GRIT’s AI chatbot exemplifies how feminist technology can advance gender equality by breaking down informational and institutional barriers for survivors of gender-based violence offering trauma-informed confidential support and connections to legal services even for those in under-resourced or rural communities.

cse3_name: Southern African Digital Inclusion Alliance (SADIA)
cse3_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse3_justif: The Southern African Digital Inclusion Alliance is a civil society initiative (CSO alliance) established in 2024 by journalists and civil society actors following a regional AI ethics and digital rights training programme. Its regular activities include public events discussions and outreach initiatives aimed at raising awareness among journalists and civil society about AI risks digital rights and the digital divide. They explicitly address Reskilling / Upskilling by strengthening the professional capacities of journalists and practitioners to understand and engage with AI and digital technologies in their work.

It also addresses AI Literacy by promoting the integration of AI into formal education including the development of interdisciplinary AI curricula across higher education and related disciplines (pp. 7 35).

cse1_name: African Hub on AI Safety Peace and Security
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_contributions: Safety and Security;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence
cse1_justif: The Global Center on AI Governance established the African Hub on AI Safety Peace and Security at the University of Cape Town. The Hub 'promotes safe and responsible AI use across Africa through collaborative research policy development and capacity building.' It works with multi-stakeholders to 'address AI-related risks that disproportionately affect marginalized groups particularly women and girls facing AI-generated harms like synthetic content and disinformation.' It offers an Academic education course in: AI Safety Honours and Technical AI Safety; as well as professional development courses. It was launched on 28 September 2025: https://ai.uct.ac.za/events/launch-african-hub-ai-safety-peace-and-security.
cse2_name: Southern African Digital Inclusion Alliance (SADIA)
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Safety and Security
cse2_justif: The Southern African Digital Inclusion Alliance is a civil society initiative (CSO alliance) established in 2024 by journalists and civil society actors following a regional AI ethics and digital rights training programme. Its regular activities include public events discussions and outreach initiatives aimed at raising awareness among journalists and civil society about AI risks digital rights and the digital divide with a particular focus on gaps in cybersecurity awareness and safe AI use. It explicitly addresses Safety and Security by strengthening the professional capacities of journalists and practitioners to understand report on and safely engage with AI including raising awareness of cybersecurity risks in their work.
cse3_name: Guidelines for Political Parties Using Generative AI
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_contributions: AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence;  Data Protection and Privacy
cse3_justif: Media Monitoring Africa (MMA) produced a set of guidelines for Political Parties using Generative AI. It emphasizes the importance of  data privacy and suggests that as a core principle 'Parties should commit to not using AI to create or propagate mis- and disinformation deepfakes and to other content designed to subvert democratic processes.' (3 Core principle 4). Principle 5 (3) also provides that 'It is often recognised that in using AI systems organisations must be careful with providing sensitive information to third-party platforms that operate AI systems in order to ensure that sources are adequately protected.'

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: Excluding Civil Society and People Affected by AI Reform  the National AI Strategy Committee
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.

cse1_justif: Five major Korean civil society organizations (Cultural Solidarity Lawyers for a Democratic Society Digital Information Committee Information and Human Rights Research Institute Progressive Network Center Participatory Solidarity) issued a joint statement criticizing the government’s National AI Strategy for being dominated by industry voices and lacking ethical academic and public representation. The statement called for stronger integration of ethics human rights and sustainability into national AI policy greater transparency and inclusion of environmental perspectives in AI governance.

cse1_justif: This report was produced by the Korean Progressive Network Jinbonet and the Institute for Digital Rights with support from the Association for Progressive Communications; these organizations are civil society organizations engaged in digital rights and public interest advocacy. The report is a critique of the Korean Basic AI Act. It talks about how children’s rights are insufficiently safeguarded warning of risks such as device dependence and privacy violations (pp. 5–7). When it comes to disability rights the Korean AI Basic Act omits explicit protections raising concerns of discrimination in welfare AI systems (pp. 9–10). Basic AI Act: https://drive.google.com/file/d/1-Q5OYGft4Qd9XqIF_3AwqOot50oEtkzi/view?usp=drive_link

cse1_name: Labor Management and Government Jointly Design Job Transition Strategies
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Labour Protections;  Reskilling and upskilling initiatives
cse1_justif: The Federation of Korean Trade Unions (FKTU) a national trade union confederation representing the collective interests of workers together with the Economic Social and Labor Council (ESLC) and the Korea Employers Federation (KEF) hosted the “AI Transition and Future of Labor Forum” on July 17 2025. The event was a public platform to gather diverse expert opinions. The forum featured a keynote speech and two sessions addressing the transformation of labor in the AI era. Speakers highlighted the need for vocational training and education a stronger social safety net to respond to AI-driven changes in employment and the need for stronger labor protection to tackle algorithm-based management and discrimination.

cse1_name: Statement of Korean Civil Society on the 2024 AI Seoul Summit

cse1_justif: In response to the Seoul Declaration held on May 21 2025 several Korean CSO groups such as Jinbonet the Institute for Digital Rights MINBYUN’s Digital Information Committee and People’s Solidarity for Participatory Democracy issued public statements and advocacy materials arguing that meaningful AI safety requires transparency independent oversight and inclusion of civil-society voices in risk assessment and monitoring.

cse1_drive: The evidence does not consist of a document.

cse1_justif: This 4-day intensive workshop was designed and facilitated by Capacity Africa Training Institute for leaders and decision-makers who seek to understand the potential of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and how to leverage it for strategic advantage. The course covered fundamental AI concepts practical applications and strategic considerations for integrating AI into business operations. Participants learnt through a mix of lectures case studies and interactive sessions gaining insights into the opportunities and challenges of AI.

cse1_name: BOSCO case
cse1_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse1_justif: The BOSCO case concerns the Spanish administration’s use of an automated system to determine eligibility for a public subsidy. Fundación Civio pursued litigation to obtain meaningful transparency about how BOSCO worked and the case culminated in a Supreme Court decision ordering disclosure of the technical information underpinning the system. This contributes to transparency and explainability by reducing citizens' uncertainty about how decision-making in welfare allocation was done and enabling scrutiny of the system used. It also supports fairness and non-discrimination indirectly: once the decision logic is accessible it becomes possible to detect and assess whether similarly situated applicants are treated consistently.
cse2_name: How to achieve real transparency through algorithm registries
cse2_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse2_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse2_justif: IA Ciudadana’s proposal on how to properly establish an effective central public algorithm registry under the AI Act promotes transparency and explainability by assisting the government in establishing a registry document what systems are used their purpose legal basis data and impacts (pp. 18–21 52). It strengthens human oversight by framing the registry as a tool for public scrutiny democratic debate and accountability enabling regulators CSOs and affected communities to monitor question and challenge algorithmic systems (pp. 12–13 41-51).
cse3_name: cAIre Project - OdysseyIA: Assessing the impact of AI externalities on society and vulnerable groups
cse3_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse3_justif: The cAIre project by OdiseIA (with support from Google foundation) is a civil society initiative that researches the impact of AI on vulnerable groups and the future of work to generate ethical and inclusive AI practices (p.3-4). It has developed resources on AI governance employment impacts inclusion and rights to support analysis of bias social risks and structural inequalities that AI systems can amplify (p.9-13). These efforts highlight how AI affects underrepresented and vulnerable groups and contribute to advancing fairness and non-discrimination by enabling evidence-based understanding of where and how AI governance should protect against discriminatory outcomes.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: OdiseIA 'Proyecto cAIre'
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Rights of Persons with Disabilities;  Gender Equality
cse1_justif: The project advocates that the technological externalities require technological solutions for their mitigation which should be addressed from the perspective of awareness and education (p. 9). Proactive regulation of algorithmic controls requires independent audits and periodic reviews to identify and mitigate deficiencies; the protection of personal data from its collection to its use by the system with special attention to the protection of data from vulnerable groups; and inclusion requirements to encompass diversity and avoid the underrepresentation of minorities (p. 10). Programs that facilitate access in remote or disconnected regions; scholarships for technology training and child protection in the digital environment (p. 11).
cse2_name: Escuela Virtual de Inteligencia Artificial
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Rights of Persons with Disabilities
cse2_justif: The ONCE program offers a Virtual AI School with free self-paced courses for people with disabilities (e.g. chatbots ChatGPT/Gemini/Copilot or Deep Learning with Python/TensorFlow). It also runs an advanced 460-hour “Programming Statistics & AI” itinerary delivered with the University of Málaga and Samsung Innovation Campus aimed at employability and culminating in projects and diplomas. The goal is to get people with disabilities involved in using and developing AI. https://web.archive.org/web/20251009121236/https://www.fundaciononce.es/es/comunicacion/noticias/un-grupo-de-personas-con-discapacidad-finaliza-un-curso-de-inteligencia

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: Conecta_IA
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives;  AI Literacy
cse1_justif: Conecta_IA is a collaboration between Fundación Esplai and Microsoft offering free online training in AI data science and cybersecurity to approximately 7500-10000 people annually in Spain. The programs are updated yearly and targets unemployed youth refugees migrants and vulnerable populations. It provides certification pathways and job orientation services. The initiative directly addresses AI literacy by training citizens in Generative AI data skills and a beter understanding of the inner workings of AI. It supports reskilling by offering on top certifications that improve employability. By focusing on vulnerable groups it democratizes access to AI competencies.
cse2_name: cAIre Project
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Labour Protections;  AI Literacy
cse2_justif: OdiseIA’s cAIre (supported by Google) is a civil-society project on AI governance support for vulnerable groups and “fair AI in the workplace” through the creation of a large public database reports and recommendations. It covers labour protection as it explicitly focuses on “fair AI in the workplace” and includes analysis of how AI transforms work and what safeguards are needed. It also covers AI literacy as it provides a documentation directory compiling guides tools case law and resources on employment impact for specific sectors helping stakeholders understand risks and responsible practices.
cse3_name: Recommendations for Collective Bargaining on AI
cse3_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse3_justif: Recommendations for Collective Bargaining on AI is a practical guide developed by trade union UGT to support workers' representatives in negotiating AI provisions in collective agreements. The guide provides a standardised template for requesting algorithmic information a decalogue of key questions and minimum content requirements unions should demand regarding AI systems affecting workplace decisions. It addresses labour protection as it tackles risks of discrimination unfair dismissal and opaque decision-making by AI systems in hiring task allocation and performance evaluation. It covers AI literacy as it equips union representatives with technical knowledge about AI and how to understand and scrutinise algorithmic systems.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Civio pulls back the curtain on public algorithms: Spain’s Supreme Court orders the Government to release BOSCO’s source code
cse1_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse1_contributions: Access to Redress and Remedy;  Data Protection and Privacy;  Data Sharing and Access
cse1_justif: The BOSCO case concerns the Spanish administration’s use of an automated system to determine eligibility for a public subsidy. Fundación Civio pursued litigation for transparency about how the system operated. The case culminated in the supreme court requiring the administration to provide access to the technical information underpinning BOSCO. This initiative advances data protection and privacy by reinforcing GDPR safeguards around automated decision-making. Supports data sharing and access giving public access to essential information about algorithmic systems used by the state enabling scrutiny. It strengthens access to redress and remedy by making it more feasible for individuals and civil society to challenge automated outcomes.
cse2_name: Proyecto cAIre
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Safety and Security;  Data Protection and Privacy;  Impact Assessments
cse2_justif: OdiseIA’s Proyecto cAIre in collaboration with Google initiative has developed guides curated documentation repositories and conceptual frameworks to help organisations and policymakers understand AI-related risks safeguards and responsible deployment practices. Therefore it contributes mainly to Safety and Security Data Protection and Privacy as it also addresses responsible data use and rights protections in AI deployment and Impact Assessments by analysing AI externalities and risks affecting particular jobs and groups.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: Conversation on the Ethics of Explainable AI
cse1_drive: This is a blog on the LIRNEasia website; no document available
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse1_justif: This roundtable discussion organised by LIRNEasia in collaboration with the United States–Sri Lanka Fulbright Commission examined explainability as essential for ensuring accountability fairness and public trust in AI. It further explored what explainability means for developers policymakers and end users emphasising the need for mechanisms such as “embedding an escape key within AI systems” or keeping a human in the loop. The discussion thereby reflected on the importance of AI transparency and ethical oversight contributing to foundational policy dialogue on explainability as a governance priority.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: Biased by Design - Chatbots and Misinformation in Sri Lanka’s 2025 Local Elections
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: This report released by Democracy Reporting International (DRI) a not-for-profit organization explicitly examines how AI-powered chatbots generate responses related to the erosion of the rights of sexual minorities highlighting bias exclusion and misrepresentation affecting the LGBTQ+ community in the Sri Lankan electoral context (pp. 9 18–24). Further it emphasises a systematic comparison of chatbot performance in English Tamil and Sinhala demonstrating inaccuracies in voter registration electoral processes and candidate information (Executive Summary pp. 2–5; Findings pp. 10–24). These findings underscore unequal access to accurate information for linguistic minorities.

cse1_drive: This is a blog post on the Commonwealth of Learning website; no document available.
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives;  AI Literacy
cse1_justif: This workshop was jointly organised by the Commonwealth Educational Media Centre for Asia (CEMCA) the Commonwealth of Learning (COL) and the Ministry of Education Sri Lanka. Its primary aim was to equip teachers with essential AI awareness through lectures demonstrations and hands-on activities. Key objectives included “understanding AI fundamentals exploring AI tools to enhance teaching and learning integrating AI into the curriculum and addressing ethical considerations in AI use. These components directly contribute to advancing AI literacy and supporting reskilling and upskilling within the education sector.
cse2_name: AI Upskilling Initiative Alumni Association of the University of Peradeniya (AAUP)
cse2_drive: This is a blog post on the AAUP webiste; no document available.
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives;  AI Literacy
cse2_justif: The AAUP in collaboration with the Lanka Education and Research Network (LEARN) launched this AI upskilling initiative to mark the opening of AI Skills Week which is “designed to empower students professionals and educators across disciplines with essential AI literacy and future-ready skills.” A panel discussion titled “Upskilling Through AI: Opportunities Realities & Roles” was conducted at the event exploring the impact of AI-driven transformation on skills development across various fields including among individuals without a technical background.
cse3_name: Algorithms at Work: The Management of Gig Work in Sri Lanka
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_justif: This report examines the proliferation of algorithms in platform-based gig work and draws on field research from Sri Lanka to analyse implications for gig workers in delivery and ride-hailing occupations. It addresses labour protections indirectly by showing how AI-driven algorithmic management affects workers’ rights autonomy transparency work conditions and access to redress (pp. 2–6). While it does not outline formal labour protections or regulatory safeguards it provides strong evidence of AI-driven labour risks and power asymmetries underscoring the urgent need for protective mechanisms in Sri Lanka’s gig economy.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Biased by Design - Chatbots and Misinformation in Sri Lanka’s 2025 Local Elections
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: In May 2025 Democracy Reporting International (DRI) a not-for-profit organization released a study examining how AI-powered chatbots generate false misleading and biased electoral information in the context of Sri Lanka’s 2025 local elections. Through trilingual testing in English Tamil and Sinhala the study documents inaccuracies in voter registration electoral processes and candidate information including partisan leanings on political issues (Executive Summary pp. 2–5; Findings pp. 10–24). These AI-generated errors pose a threat to electoral integrity and democratic participation prompting recommendations for mitigation measures such as limiting chatbot responses and directing voters to official sources (pp. 25-26).

cse1_name: Position Paper on the Regulation of Automated Decision-Making Systems
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_contributions: Human Oversight and Determination;  Transparency and Explainability;  Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Environmental Impact
cse1_justif: The Digital Society creates policy papers and is involved in actively and officially giving advice to the Swiss government but also the European Union. This initiative (the publication I attached as evidence) sets out to protect against non-discrimination (page 9) and requires ADM systems to be transparent and explainable to humans (page 7 pages 12-13). It is human-centered retaining human sovereignty over the machine (pages 4 7) and notes that a future task is the continuous assessment of technology impacts on the natural and social environment (page 16).
cse2_name: Women in Digital: Navigating Digital Transformation and AI
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse2_justif: This is a community of leaders to leverage collaboration and promote diversity to boost careers and create business opportunities in the Swiss digital economy. Speakers explored how they are navigating digital transformation integrating AI into their businesses and reshaping their respective sectors. They also discussed the unique challenges women face in technology and entrepreneurship offering practical advice and strategies for success.
cse3_name: How and Why Algorithms Discriminate
cse3_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse3_justif: The article addresses non-discrimination in the 'Explainer' column which provides examples of discrimination in other countries such as the Netherlands and Germany. 'How and why algorithms discriminate' serves to engage and raise awareness emphasizing: 'It is difficult to identify the people affected since they generally do not know they are being discriminated against by automated decisions.' It details the causes such as data and developer bias in the section 'What are the causes of algorithmic discrimination?'. It offers examples in the 'Case studies of algorithmic discrimination' and concludes that the Swiss non-discrimination law (Art. 8 Abs. 2 BV) is insufficient against private agents in the section Swiss law on discrimination.
cse4_name: Position paper: On the regulation of automated decision-making systems
cse4_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse4_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse4_justif: The report notes that current legislation in Switzerland offers insufficient protection to deal with automated decision-making systems. The organization presents a proposed legal framework to address AI systems based on three pillars: the protection of individuals and society must be guaranteed; the classification of risk systems—whether low risk high risk or unacceptable risk—by self-declaration; companies and public authorities must assume full responsibility for any damages caused by the use of systems; systems used by the public sector must meet even more stringent supervision and sanction requirements.
cse5_name: Digital Trust Criteria Swiss Digital Initiative
cse5_justif: The Digital Trust Label (DTL) was launched in January 2022. The label has been updated in its criteria to include AI. With this updated Criteria Catalog the label fills the existing gap between the growing demand for AI and the constantly evolving regulations. The label functions as an audit and allows companies to move forward with the implementation of AI in a reliable way focusing on four areas: Security Data Protection Reliability and Fair User Interaction (p.3). Version 3 valid as of June 2024 includes AI. (https://web.archive.org/web/20250504092930/https://swiss-digital-initiative.org/news/4148-2/)

cse1_name: Gender Equality and Digital Transformation (EGTN) project
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: The “Gender Equality and Digital Transformation” project was a collaborative project funded by 'swissuniversities' as part of the Programme 'Diversity Inclusion and Equal Opportunities in the University Development'. It was led by the University of Lausanne in collaboration with EPFL HES-SO and the strukturelle association (a private association working in the area of gender equality promotion in the workspace). The objectives of the project were: to draw a state of the art on the mutual effects of digital transformation and gender bias to encourage innovative approaches using digital technology to promote gender equality  and to study concrete ways of supporting women’s careers in the digital sector (p.4-5).
