cse1_name: Employee Representative Participation in the Use of Automated Decision-Making Systems
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: Project between AlgorithmWatch CH and Syndicom for a basic analysis of the use of automated decision-making systems in the Swiss world of work. It aims to develop qualification concepts for workers' representatives so that they can guarantee effective participation. It is an analysis of the political and legal action necessary to guarantee the prerequisites for workers' participation rights and the structural conditions for interventions by unions and workers' representatives. Basic analysis of the use of automated decision-making (ADM) systems in the Swiss world of work (p. 4).
cse2_name: The New Swiss AI Diploma. Development of professional exams or advanced professional exams in the field of AI.
cse2_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse2_contributions: AI Literacy;  Reskilling and upskilling initiatives
cse2_justif: In May 2024 the Swiss Association for ICT Vocational Training analyzed the labor market and business needs. The results clearly demonstrated a high demand and an urgent need for advanced formal training qualifications in the labor market. Therefore in collaboration with representatives of Swiss industry and with the financial support of Huawei the association launched an educational project to develop a new federal advanced vocational qualification in AI. The New Swiss AI Diploma aims at developing professional examinations or higher professional examinations in the field of AI. Autumn 2025: Start of the first programs. The goal is to place the first graduates in the labor market in 2026.
cse3_name: Swiss AI Weeks
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_justif: By combining research and practice the Swiss AI Weeks invite researchers developers entrepreneurs and all interested parties to collaboratively shape the future of AI. In total there were: 240 events around AI; 15 Hackathons – 12-48 hour hacks to tackle challenges in health finance gov-tech and industry; Expert events – lightning talks deep dives and panels; Public sessions – demos crash courses and art & AI meet-ups; Startup boost – winning hacks enter Kickstart's incubation track; and Education formats – hands-on online formats that make AI easy for everyone. For instance Coding with AI: What You Need to Learn Unlearn and Rethink or  95% of AI pilots fail. How to be in the 5%? — An Interactive Learning Session.
cse4_name: Algorithms in the Workplace: Empowering Employees – Enabling Participation
cse4_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse4_contributions: Labour Protections
cse4_justif: This publication is primarily intended for trade unions to defend workers' rights when algorithmic systems are used as they can represent the collective interests and responsibilities necessary to shape these transformation processes. It reinforces basic notions about the systems and applicable legislation basic ethical principles and measures to demand worker participation in different phases of algorithmic system development (pg.2)

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: An analysis of Microsoft’s Bing Chat. Generative AI and elections: are chatbots a reliable source of information for voters?
cse1_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse1_contributions: Safety and Security;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence
cse1_justif: The report presents a joint investigation by AlgorithmWatch and AI Forensics into Bing Chat the AI-powered chatbot of Microsoft's Bing search engine. The chatbot fabricates false scandals about real politicians and manipulates election poll numbers. The report and tests show that the chatbot is an unreliable source for potential voters seeking information about upcoming elections (p. 20). For a voter a search engine or chatbot with generative AI is not a reliable or transparent source of information (p. 21). Generative models can pose a risk to candidates' reputations and spread false information about them (p. 21).
cse2_name: Swiss Digital Initiative: Towards trustworthy AI in digital services
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Safety and Security;  Consumer Protection
cse2_justif: Swiss Digital Initiative is a non-profit foundation headquartered in Geneva. They manage the Digital Trust Label and are responsible for the expertise behind the label and the final decision to award the label in collaboration with the Board. In the file I share they introduce a self-assessment tool for Digital Service Providers. This tool addresses specifically AI security data protection AI reliability and fair user interaction. (pages 6-10)

cse1_name: Report 'Balancing progress and human rights:Is Thailand ready for Artificial Intelligencethat respects human rights?' by Manushya Foundation Thomson Reuters Foundation and Trust Law
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_contributions: Gender Equality;  Children’s Rights;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse1_justif: The report provides evidence of CSO engagement by offering critiques information and recommendations on Thailand’s AI strategies (NAIS) and Ethics Guidelines. It highlights four concerns: Gender equality – the AI Ethics Guideline does not address issues affecting LGBTQ+ individuals (p.35). Cultural diversity – it fails to include protections against AI-related harms to indigenous and ethnic minority groups (p.35). Children’s rights – facial recognition was used to randomly collect children’s data later expanding in the Deep South (pp.16–17). People with disabilities – the report urges Parliament to propose an algorithmic accountability bill to prevent discrimination (p.48).

cse1_name: Seminar 'Time for Thailand to establish guidelines for governing AI' by the Thai Journalists Association the Online News Producers Association the Faculty of Communication Arts at Chulalongkorn University Cofact Thailand and Thai PBS.
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_contributions: AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence;  Consumer Protection
cse1_justif: Mr Jeerapong Prasertpolkrang Secretary-General of the Thai Journalists Association cautioned that news credibility is at risk when AI is used to transcribe documents without proper verification leading to AI-facilitated misinformation. Audiences may not be aware that AI-generated outputs can contain errors. He further emphasised that relying solely on prompts to obtain statistics without checking original sources seriously undermines accuracy and raises consumer protection concerns. These remarks were made during a seminar jointly organised by the National Press Council of Thailand and the Thai Journalists Association both acting as non-state actors.

The event raised public awareness on the ethics and governance of AI use (Day 1 as seen on the website) promoted inclusive dialogue among citizens experts and policymakers and encouraged human-centered oversight of AI development. It is unclear from the program presentation what ethical aspects were covered exactly.

cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: CybersafeTT is a registered  CSO operating in Trinidad and Tobago.  It responded to Her Excellency President Christine Kangaloo’s call for swift action on artificial intelligence (AI) legislation. This CSO initiative specifically addresses AI trust and safety by: Identifying gaps in current legislation regarding AI systems; Calling for AI-specific accountability transparency and fairness measures; Addressing AI-driven harms (algorithmic discrimination misinformation deepfakes); Advocating for enforceable data protection in AI contexts; Demanding redress mechanisms for AI-related consumer harm.

cse1_name: Journalism and AI :Issues and Opportunities of Artificial Intelligence in Tunisia
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse1_justif: The 2025 PAMT2 / CFI Médias report Journalism and AI: Challenges and Opportunities in Tunisia highlights the importance of developing an ethical charter for AI use in journalism. In Section 1.4 – Recommendations (pp. 22–23) it calls for clear ethical principles governing AI in media including algorithmic transparency accountability human oversight and respect for journalistic integrity. The report presents the ethical charter as a key mechanism to reduce risks of bias opacity and misinformation in AI-assisted news production while strengthening public trust and professional responsibility within Tunisia’s media ecosystem.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: ALECSO Charter on Artificial Intelligence Ethics
cse1_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse1_contributions: Gender Equality;  Children’s Rights;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse1_justif: The ALECSO Charter on AI Ethics (2025) explicitly addresses inclusion and diversity. In “Justice Inclusiveness Equity and Non-Discrimination” (p. 14) it mandates equal access to AI regardless of gender disability religion or socio-economic status and requires prevention of algorithmic bias with redress mechanisms. In “Cultural Diversity and Inclusion” (p. 15) it affirms that AI must respect Arab cultural and social diversity and ensure accessible participation for all communities. These provisions embed human rights cultural sensitivity and equity in AI governance across the Arab region including Tunisia.

cse1_name: Civil Society Advocacy Against Tunisia's Biometric ID and Digital Transformation Programs
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Impact Assessments;  Consumer Protection
cse1_justif: Access Now and Tunisian CSOs advocated against biometric ID legislation from November 2023 through March 2024 demanding human rights safeguards for AI-enabled digital identity systems. Despite Parliament adopting bills in March 2024 CSOs continue advocating. Data Protection: Highlighted outdated 2004 legislation inadequate for biometric systems and lack of transparency in data collection/storage. Safety & Security: Warned of mass surveillance identity theft and data exploitation risks from weak cybersecurity protections. Consumer Protection: Demanded transparency in how citizen biometric data would be collected used and protected.
cse2_name: Digital Trust & Security Days 2024: Arab Regional Forum on AI and Cybersecurity Capacity Building
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Safety and Security;  Impact Assessments
cse2_justif: Held in Tunis on December 3–4 2024 the Digital Trust & Security Days were co-organized by the Arab Information and Communication Technologies Organization (AICTO) — a civil-society–oriented intergovernmental body — in partnership with Tunisia’s Ministry of Communication Technologies and the National Cybersecurity Agency (ANC). AICTO acted as the primary organizer facilitating regional capacity building on cybersecurity data protection and AI risk governance. The event strengthened technical cooperation and institutional knowledge across Arab states contributing to a safer human-centred digital ecosystem.
cse3_name: Student Fact-Checking Network (SFCN) – Tunisia chapter
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_justif: The Student Fact-Checking Network (SFCN) engages the Tunisian public by producing accessible fact-checks explainer videos and awareness campaigns that help citizens identify AI-generated misinformation. By publishing verification content across social media and collaborating with universities nationwide SFCN reaches wider youth communities beyond journalism students. The initiative promotes public awareness encourages civic participation in reporting false content and strengthens societal resilience to AI-driven disinformation.

cse1_name: Improving the Competitiveness of Women in the Private Sector of the Labor Market of Turkmenistan
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: The Union of Economists of Turkmenistan (supported by the EU) conducted a series of training on enhancing women’s entrepreneurial skills which included sessions on the use of innovative technologies and AI. Trainings were organized in Ashgabat and Turkmenbashy in August 2025 (12-13 15-16 and 22-23) reaching 40 female participants. Sessions were led by certified professionals in relevant fields. However there is no detailed information available online regarding the contents of AI-related sessions their quality who it was led by and whether they included issues of gender equality in the context of AI. https://web.archive.org/save/https://orient.tm/en/post/88211/new-stage-womens-entrepreneurship-support-program-starts-turkmenistan
cse2_name: Launch of the speech synthesizer in the Turkmen language for people with visual impairments
cse2_type: Other
cse2_contributions: Rights of Persons with Disabilities
cse2_justif: The launch event introducing Dunya a speech synthesizer in the Turkmen language for people with visual impairments to expand educational opportunities for children and adults with visual impairments. The program was developed jointly by the Ministry of Education ATScale Global Partnership for Assistive Technologies the Society of the Blind and Deaf of Turkmenistan (NGO) Louder Pages and UNICEF Turkmenistan. The Society of the Blind and Deaf of Turkmenistan was closely consulted to ensure the needs of the visually impaired community were properly addressed when developing the software. The event was attended by users of the software representatives of the government educational institutions and national media.

cse1_name: Data Ladies
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_justif: Data Ladies is a monthly initiative by Pollicy a non-profit organization following the success of DataFest Africa. It fosters a community for data professionals in Uganda and East Africa specifically promoting women's leadership in the industry. Sessions cover data science machine learning and storytelling. Pollicy works to integrate AI into the feminist movement by advocating for responsible data governance and policies through engagement with women’s groups and gender minorities. This project directly supports gender inclusion and capacity building in the Uganda AI ecosystem

cse1_name: Webinar on Strengthening AI Governance in Uganda
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_contributions: Safety and Security;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence;  Impact Assessments
cse1_justif: The Centre for Multilateral Affairs (CfMA) a Ugandan CSO held a webinar on 28 Jan. 2025 with stakeholders titled “Strengthening AI Governance in Uganda” discussing trust safety regulatory gaps such as algorithmic bias and surveillance. Webinar participants discussed; the State of AI Governance in Uganda key challenges proposed solutions recommendations and agreed on the way-forward for different  actors: government private sector & CSO. Some of the key recommendations given are: develop AI-Specific Legislation Strengthen Transparency and AccountabilityInvest in AI Education and Capacity Building and Review and Amend Existing Digital Policies.

cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination

cse2_name: Online training “Copyright for media: practical tips and protection algorithms”

Also it provides guidance on adequate labelling of AI outputs (which is one of the constituents of transparency standard) to ensure compliance with IP regulations.
cse3_name: Virtuous Media: How to Preserve Democracy in the Digital World — Public Broadcasting Conference
cse3_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse3_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse3_justif: One of the panels within the conference was explicitly devoted to ensuring transparency and oversight in AI use among the media actors. The conference itself was co-organised by the Public Broadcaster and the CSO Centre for Democracy and Rule of Law.
cse4_name: Adapting EU Artificial Intelligence Regulations for Electoral Processes: A Path for Ukraine
cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse4_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse4_justif: The research report explores the legal frameworks applicable to AI systems used within the electoral processes highlighting the need for transparency equity and due oversight.
cse5_name: Human-Rights-Compliant Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems: Toolkit for Civil Society
cse5_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse5_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse5_justif: The Toolkit provides the guidelines on responsible selection and deployment of AI systems ensuring due human oversight and transparency requirements particularly through the requirements of labelling and disclosure.

cse1_name: Expert Committee on Development of AI in Ukraine under the Ministry for Digital Transformation of Ukraine
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Cultural and Linguistic Diversity;  Gender Equality
cse1_justif: The Committee comprises multiple experts from civil society sector (Olga Petriv from CEDEM Tetiana Avdieieva from DSLU) who are consulting the Ministry on formulation of the public policy in the AI sector including contributions to the sectoral recommendations addressing the issues of gender equality and diversity among various spheres of AI application. For example the Committee participated in drafting recommendations on responsible AI for developers that addresses both aspects (https://web.archive.org/web/20250924091008/https://thedigital.gov.ua/storage/uploads/files/page/community/docs/%D0%A8%D0%86%20%D0%B4%D0%BB%D1%8F%20%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B7%D1%80%D0%BE%D0%B1%D0%BD%D0%B8%D0%BA%D1%96%D0%B2.pdf?utm_id=1)
cse2_name: Artificial Intelligence in Media Work: OSINT Tools Fact-Checking Policies and Protection from Online Attacks
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Gender Equality
cse2_justif: The webinar organised by an NGO 'Women in Media' was devoted to mapping of threats posed by AI for Ukrainian female journalists and the potential tools to tackle negative effects. It was directed to journalists to increase their knowledge on how to turn AI into an assistant rather than a threat.
cse3_name: Survey of Ukrainian media professionals on threats posed by artificial intelligence (AI)
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_justif: The survey has been conducted to collect data related to gender based violence caused by AI among Ukrainian media professionals and to raise awareness regarding the threats posed by AI in this area. It was accompanied by the raise awareness campaign on AI threats and challenges and community building follow-up event.
cse4_name: “I saw you in porn how could you be a journalist”: how gender AI-driven misinformation works
cse4_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse4_contributions: Gender Equality
cse4_justif: The research prepared by the NGO 'Women in Media' within the framework of the project 'Network of Gender Think Tanks: Strengthening Capacity for Advanced Policy Development Impact Assessment Strategic Advocacy and Focused Policy Communications' explores the impact of the AI-driven gender based disinformation campaigns on the rights of female journalists
cse5_name: Human-Rights-Compliant Use of Artificial Intelligence Systems: Toolkit for Civil Society
cse5_contributions: Gender Equality;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse5_justif: The toolkit provides a guidance for civil society on how to responsibly choose and deploy AI systems avoiding discrimination bias and stereotypes including in the areas of gender equality and safeguarding the rights of other vulnerable and/or marginalised groups. It was developed with assistance of CSO experts addressing all peculiarities of CSO and human rights defenders work including tips for advocacy in the AI sphere.

cse3_name: The media literacy index of Ukrainians remains high but the level of digital competence has decreased over the year according to a study by 'Media Detector'

cse1_name: WISP GCC Local Group Event: Women at the Forefront of Responsible AI in Privacy and Security
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_contributions: Safety and Security;  Data Protection and Privacy
cse1_justif: The Women in Security & Privacy (WISP) GCC event “Women at the Forefront of Responsible AI in Privacy & Security” (held January 2025 at Meta Dubai Internet City) directly addressed both privacy and security in the context of AI adoption.The discussion centred on how AI systems process personal and sensitive data highlighting data minimisation governance and accountability principles to prevent misuse. It also explored AI-driven cybersecurity risks such as model manipulation and data breaches promoting best practices for secure AI deployment responsible data handling and inclusive workforce participation to strengthen digital trust.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: UK’s First Legal Challenge to a Data Centre – Foxglove & Global Action Plan
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_justif: The legal challenge by Foxglove and Global Action Plan addresses Environmental Impact by opposing a hyperscale data centre's construction without an Environmental Impact Assessment. The case highlights projected high energy and water consumption threats to local resources and conflict with UK climate goals. Filed August 2025 it aims to set precedent for holding data centre projects accountable to environmental standards. Note: The page refers to 'hyperscale data centres' rather than 'AI infrastructure' though linked articles reference AI datacentres. See: https://www.foxglove.org.uk/2025/08/21/new-case-launch-uk-first-legal-challenge-data-centre/
cse2_name: Data and AI are changing our world
cse2_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse2_contributions: Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse2_justif: The activity led by the UK CSO Connected by Data as outlined in its 2024–25 Annual Report focuses on strengthening public and worker participation and collaboration in AI governance. It addresses Fairness and Non-Discrimination by empowering communities and civil society actors to influence decisions on data and AI ensuring more inclusive and equitable outcomes. It also addresses Human Oversight and Determination by promoting public and worker involvement in AI-related decision-making including collaboration with unions and government bodies to embed participatory approaches in public sector AI governance.
cse3_name: Stop Facial Recognition Campaign – Big Brother Watch
cse3_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse3_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability
cse3_justif: The “Stop Facial Recognition” campaign by Big Brother Watch addresses fairness transparency  by exposing racial bias in facial recognition systems which disproportionately misidentify Black and minority individuals. It challenges the opaque deployment of this technology demanding algorithmic accountability public transparency and legal safeguards. The campaign calls for a ban on public use and promotes democratic oversight to prevent rights violations and systemic discrimination.
cse4_name: Exploring Local Authority Uses of Data Analytics and AI – Ada Lovelace Institute
cse4_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse4_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse4_justif: For Transparency and Explainability the report finds that clear system outputs and understandable explanations are critical for building trust; workers are more likely to rely on AI when decisions are transparent and interpretable (Insights 4-5). For Human Oversight and Determination the report emphasizes that data analytics should support not replace professional judgment. Frontline workers must verify outputs before acting alerts serve as prompts for further human investigation and staff retained authority to override system recommendations. The Recommendations section calls for mechanisms enabling human review where data-informed decisions produce undesirable outcomes. See Insights 3-5 and Conclusions.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: 5Rights Foundation Advocacy
cse1_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse1_justif: The 5Rights Foundation is a CSO that participates in public policy formulation on children’s rights in digital environments. It has directly contributed to the development and shaping of key regulatory frameworks on AI including the UK Age Appropriate Design Code (in force since 2021) and has actively engaged in policy processes related to the EU Digital Services Act (DSA) and the EU AI Act (2023–2024). It also contributed to the UN Committee on the Rights of the Child General Comment No. 25 on children’s rights in the digital environment (https://5rightsfoundation.com/our-work/advocacy/).
cse2_name: Gender and AI (Workshop)
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Gender Equality
cse2_justif: The activity led by the Ada Lovelace Institute is a completed Gender and AI project that included a workshop with transgender and non-binary participants. It addresses Gender Equality by examining how data-driven systems reinforce binary gender assumptions and exclude gender-diverse individuals resulting in unequal treatment and access to healthcare. The workshop centres lived experiences to identify these inequalities and inform recommendations for more inclusive system design that better reflects diverse gender identities.
cse3_name: EdTech and the Future of Education
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_justif: The initiative focuses on safeguarding children's rights in the context of educational technologies and AI. It generates evidence on how AI-driven EdTech affects children's development privacy and participation rights. It engages young people directly in shaping research and policy blueprints and advocates for rights-based digital education systems through workshops consultations and publications. By producing rigorous research on EdTech impacts and informing policy frameworks the initiative helps identify and mitigate AI-based harms such as discriminatory algorithmic profiling privacy violations and developmentally inappropriate design in educational settings.
cse4_name: Children & AI Design Code: A protocol for the development and use of AI systems that impact children
cse4_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse4_contributions: Children’s Rights
cse4_justif: The Code embeds children's rights across the AI lifecycle. Part 3 Criteria 3.1–3.9 (pp. 21–22) require developmentally appropriate design fairness transparency accountability and upholding UNCRC rights including best interests and participation. Section 5.8 (pp. 44–47) mandates child-friendly reporting easy appeals (including without logging in) and human contact for remedy. Section 2.7 (p. 20) requires additive protections recognising intersectional vulnerabilities. The Transparency case study (p. 87) operationalises these principles by requiring 'a full review of all harms a child may be exposed to taking into account their age and intersectional vulnerabilities' with age-restriction required.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: TUC Trades Union Congress AI Bill Project
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: The Trades Union Congress AI Bill Project developed the draft Artificial Intelligence (Regulation and Employment Rights) Bill to protect workers from high-risk AI decisions. Key provisions include: Workplace AI Risk Assessments (WAIRA) with mandatory consultation (p.6); statutory union consultation rights before AI deployment (p.6); algorithmic transparency and right to human review (p.6); union right to data access (p.7); protection against discriminatory algorithms with reversed burden of proof (p.7); right to disconnect (p.8); and protection against unfair dismissal by AI (p.8). The Bill was developed through a Special Advisory Committee with unions academia civil society and cross-party MPs meeting three times in 2023-24 (p.4).
cse2_name: App Drivers & Couriers Union and Equality and Human Rights Commission Legal Action Against Uber Eats for Discriminatory AI Facial Recognition
cse2_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse2_contributions: Labour Protections
cse2_justif: The App Drivers & Couriers Union and Equality and Human Rights Commission filed legal action on behalf of Pa Edrissa Manjang an Uber Eats courier removed from the platform after AI facial recognition checks found 'continued mismatches' (p.2). The case addressed how AI and automated processes could 'permanently suspend a driver's access to the app depriving them of an income' without transparency or effective challenge routes (p.2).  The case was 'among the first to consider AI and automated decision making in the context of work and the potential for unfairness and discrimination' (p.5). (page numbers from drive pdf)
cse3_name: Good Things Foundation AI Literacy Programme
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_justif: For Reskilling/Upskilling the initiative provides training for community organisation staff including 'AI tools to support the running of your organisation' and 'Effective prompt crafting' (p.26). Recommendations emphasize embedding 'AI support as part of existing digital skills learning' for practical tasks like writing CVs letter templates and language translation (p.24).
cse4_name: The Experience AI Challenge
cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse4_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives;  AI Literacy
cse4_justif: The Experience AI Challenge addresses AI Literacy by providing structured curriculum-aligned resources that teach students core AI concepts through hands-on project-based learning in schools. It also supports reskilling/upskilling by equipping teachers with mentor guides training materials and webinars that build their capacity to understand and teach AI effectively. The initiative is led by the Raspberry Pi Foundation in collaboration with Google DeepMind. It contributes to public awareness and engagement through open-access resources live webinars and public showcase events which involve students educators and wider communities and promote broader understanding of AI and its real-world implications.
cse5_name: Education and AI: The role of AI and data-driven technologies in primary and secondary education in the UK
cse5_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse5_justif: The project “Education and AI: The role of AI and data-driven technologies in primary and secondary education in the UK” is a CSO-led knowledge generation activity conducted by the Ada Lovelace Institute in collaboration with the Nuffield Foundation. It generates evidence-based insights on how AI is used in schools including its impact on teaching assessment and student outcomes as can be seen in their accompanying publication (p. 5) 'A learning curve? A Iandscape review of AI and education in the UK' https://www.adalovelaceinstitute.org/wp-content/uploads/2025/01/Ada-Lovelace-Institute-Nuffield-Foundation-A-learning-curve.pdf?v=1757593240.

country: United Kingdom of Great Britain and Northern Ireland
indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: https://connectedbydata.org/projects/2024-jrrt-influencing
cse1_link: Seizing The Moment- Influencing UK data policy
cse1_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse1_justif: The activity led by the UK CSO Connected by Data (“Seizing the Moment: Influencing UK Data Policy”) focuses on participation in public policy formulation. It seeks to influence UK AI and data governance by shaping political party manifestos advocating amendments or opposition to the Data Protection and Digital Information Bill and supporting policymakers through civil society input. It explicitly addresses Data Protection and Privacy through its focus on this legislation specifically in light of AI technologies influence in various sectors.
cse2_name: Legal Challenge to Live Facial Recognition – Big Brother Watch & Shaun Thompson
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Safety and Security;  Access to Redress and Remedy
cse2_justif: Big Brother Watch a UK civil liberties organization is bringing this legal challenge against the Met Police's use of live facial recognition technology. The case contests privacy violations from scanning and storing biometric data without consent and raises safety concerns over racially biased AI in public surveillance. Big Brother Watch represents claimants including a man wrongly flagged and detained highlighting the absence of redress for such harms. Through this litigation the organization seeks to establish legal limits and safeguards for police use of facial recognition technology.
cse3_name: Data and AI Civil Society Network
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Data Sharing and Access
cse3_justif: The activity led by the UK CSO Data and AI Civil Society Network falls under Capacity building and collaboration as it is a structured network that brings together civil society organisations researchers unions and campaigners to exchange knowledge coordinate activities and build collective expertise on data and AI governance through regular meetings and working groups. It explicitly addresses Data Protection and Privacy by facilitating collaboration and resource sharing on data protection policy and Data Sharing and Access by enabling coordinated engagement on data governance issues across participating organisations.
cse4_name: Trust and Truth in the Age of AI – Full Fact Report 2024
cse4_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse4_contributions: Impact Assessments;  Safety and Security;  Consumer Protection;  Access to Redress and Remedy;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence
cse4_justif: Full Fact’s “Trust and Truth in the Age of AI” initiative proposes ethical standards to counter AI-driven misinformation focusing on safety impact assessments and remedies. It recommends mandatory transparency for AI systems used in elections (p. 14) stronger consumer protections against deceptive AI content (p. 13) mechanisms for correcting false claims (p. 11) and robust evaluations of AI’s societal impact (p. 9). The report directly tackles risks from generative AI and misinformation.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: The United Republic of Tanzania Artificial Intelligence Readiness Assessment Report
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_contributions: Human Oversight and Determination;  Environmental Impact;  Transparency and Explainability;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse1_justif: The report addresses the four initiatives. 1. Fairness & Non-Discrimination: identifies algorithmic bias as a key ethical risk that could deepen inequalities (p. 36) and mandates bias audits and ethical certifications for public sector AI procurement (p. 60) to prevent unfair outcomes. 2. Transparency: ensures openness of AI systems’ internal mechanisms through algorithmic impact assessments and transparency regulations (p. 60) documenting models datasets logic and rationale reinforced by the Access to Information Act (pp. 26–27) for public scrutiny. 3. Human Oversight: promotes ethics boards and human-centered design (pp. 16 36 60–61 65). 4. Environmental Impact: supports sustainable AI and renewable data centers (pp. 32 62).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: Consultations for AI Readiness Assessment in Tanzania
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Gender Equality;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse1_justif: The report addresses cultural and linguistic diversity by recommending Kiswahili NLP development and culturally contextual AI policies. It addresses gender equality by identifying gaps in digital access and STEM participation and proposing measures such as scholarships mentorship and inclusive education programs to empower women in AI. Furthermore it acknowledges the critical role of the Tanzania AI Community a civil society organization in driving the national AI readiness assessment. This CSO facilitated the deployment of UNESCO’s RAM coordinated multi-stakeholder consultations across three regions and ensured inclusive participation from over 240 stakeholders embedding diverse perspectives into Tanzania’s AI strategy.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: dLab — Mahadia Tunga WiDS Fellowship
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: The dLab – Mahadia Tunga WiDS Fellowship addresses capacity building and collaboration by training Tanzanian students especially women in AI and data science through an MSc program at UDSM. Fellows engage in real-world research in-residence work at dLab and mentorship developing practical skills in data analytics visualization and machine learning. The initiative also provides workshops bootcamps and collaborative projects fostering networks and knowledge exchange.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Jamii Check
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_justif: JamiiCheck is a Tanzanian fact-checking platform run by JamiiForums that combines expert analysis community input and AI tools to fight disinformation (see example in the provided screenshot - AI-generated false video). It verifies the authenticity of news images and videos including checking whether content has been generated or manipulated by artificial intelligence. By detecting AI-made or altered media and clarifying misleading claims JamiiCheck helps citizens distinguish real information from synthetic or deceptive content promoting digital literacy accountability and trust in public discourse.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: ACLU et al. v. NSA (FOIA lawsuit)
cse1_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse1_justif: ACLU et al. v. NSA (FOIA lawsuit filed May 13 2024) addresses Transparency and Explainability by seeking disclosure of government records concerning the NSA’s use of artificial intelligence including how AI systems are deployed governed and assessed enabling public understanding and scrutiny of AI-enabled surveillance (pp. 3 7). It also warns against using algorithmic systems to gather and analyze intelligence as it can compound privacyintrusions and perpetuate discrimination (p. 7).
cse2_name: Teaching Responsible Computing Playbook
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse2_justif: The Mozilla Foundation’s Responsible AI Playbook addresses Fairness and Non-Discrimination by providing practical guidance on inclusive design bias identification and harm mitigation across the AI lifecycle (see sections on “Equity and Fairness” and “Avoiding Algorithmic Harm”). It supports Transparency and Explainability through recommendations on documentation disclosure of system limitations and clear communication with affected users (sections on “Transparency” and “Explainability”). The playbook advances Human Oversight and Determination by emphasizing governance structures accountability and human-in-the-loop decision-making in AI development and deployment (sections on “Accountability” and “Human Control and Governance”).
cse3_name: Comply to fly? How Airport Travelers Experience TSA´s Facial Recognition Experiment
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_contributions: Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability
cse3_justif: The Algorithmic Justice League (AJL) “Freedom Flyers” campaign and “Comply To Fly?” report address Fairness and Non-Discrimination by documenting racial bias risks and discriminatory impacts of TSA facial recognition on travelers (Comply To Fly? report sections on disparate impacts and traveler testimonies pp. 6–12). They advance Transparency and Explainability by informing the public about how facial recognition is deployed clarifying notice and consent practices and explaining opt-out rights under Section B. PROGRAM TRANSPARENCY.
cse4_name: Policy Alignment on AI Transparency: Analyzing interoperability of documentation requirements across eight frameworks.
cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse4_contributions: Transparency and Explainability
cse4_justif: The Partnership on AI (PAI) report Policy Alignment on AI Transparency (2024) addresses Transparency and Explainability by analyzing and comparing documentation requirements (e.g. model cards datasheets transparency reports) across U.S. EU UK and multilateral AI governance frameworks emphasizing interoperable documentation as a key tool for accountability and explainability (pp. 1–8 13–15) showing how consistent documentation enables regulatory supervision audits civil society scrutiny and informed human decision-making across jurisdictions including through AI Safety Institutes and oversight bodies (pp. 4–6 14–15 37–39).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: ACLU Sues Intuit and HireVue Over Discriminatory AI Interviewing Practices
cse1_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse1_contributions: Rights of Persons with Disabilities;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse1_justif: American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU)'s complaint alleges that HireVue’s AI video interview and ASR-based scoring disadvantaged a Deaf applicant that some audible content lacked subtitles and that a request for human-generated captioning was denied. On pp.4–5 (FACTUAL BACKGROUND ¶14–20) it explains the tool’s pipeline (ASR ? meaning model ? ML scoring) and why this can be discriminatory for Deaf speech. On p.9 (¶48–52) it alleges a failure to provide reasonable accommodation under ADA/CADA.Cultural & Linguistic Diversity: On p.1 n.1 the complaint describes Deaf culture/ASL as a “sociolinguistic community.” On pp.2–3 (¶4) it states that ASR systems perform worse for Indigenous dialect.
cse2_name: Comments to the United States Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP) on An Artificial Intelligence (AI) Action Plan
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Gender Equality;  Children’s Rights
cse2_justif: The Center for AI Digital Policy (CAIDP) issued comments to the United States Office of Science and Technology Policy (OSTP).  Gender Equality: The discussion appears in the “Algorithmic Fairness” section on PDF p.12/13 where it cites reports of “ChatGPT’s bias towards women” and recommends validation for fairness and transparency plus ex-ante assessments before models are released/marketed. In relation to Children’s Rights: The discussion appears in “6) Public Safety” on PDF p.7/13 stating that AI services will “turbocharge” risks including “online child safety” and calling for red lines and safety oversight for advanced AI systems.
cse3_name: Snapchot: Civil Rigths AI and Privacy
cse3_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse3_contributions: Gender Equality;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities
cse3_justif: The Briefer by the Center forCivil Rights and Technology addresses Gender Equality and Disability Rights by urging civil-rights safeguards in privacy/AI rules. In IV. Deep Dive: Comprehensive Privacy Legislation (pp.7–8) it highlights a nondiscrimination provision barring data practices that deny equal enjoyment of goods/services on the basis of “sex” and “disability” (and other protected classes). It also discusses requirements for companies to assess “covered algorithms” used for “consequential decisions” including evaluations/audits to detect discriminatory impacts (same section).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: EEOC priorities on automated systems and technological discrimination
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: The letter from ACLU Center for Democracy & Technology Governing for Impact National Women’s Law Center NAACP Legal Defense and Educational Fund and Upturn urges the EEOC to strengthen worker protections against “technological discrimination” from automated decision systems (ADS) used in hiring/management. It recommends (i) worker-facing “know your rights” fact sheets on ADS/ESAM and accommodations/notice duties (Section I.A pp. 2–3) and (ii) expanded employer guidance and formal rules clarifying obligations under Title VII/ADA/PWFA (Section I.B and I.B.1 pp. 3–4).
cse2_name: AUTHORS GUILD vs OpenAI
cse2_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse2_contributions: Labour Protections
cse2_justif: The lawsuit by the AUTHORS GUILD seeks labour/economic protections for professional authors by challenging alleged unauthorized use of their works to train LLMs that “endanger… ability to make a living” and “harm the market” for their writing (2–3 p.1). It details impacts on authors’ livelihoods and lost writing income (“GPT-N’s and ChatGPT’s Harm to Authors” 120–124 pp.15–19) and requests damages and injunctive relief to prevent recurring harm (6 p.2; Prayer for Relief p.46) and undermining fair practices in the profession.
cse3_name: Shaping AI's impact on quality jobs and economic advancement.
cse3_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse3_justif: JFF’s Center for AI & the Future of Work explicitly supports AI literacy and upskilling/reskilling for workforce and education actors. Under “Preparing the Field” it states it helps practitioners “improve their AI literacy and fluency” and adapt programs/services (Key Initiatives ? Preparing the Field lines 66–68). For upskilling/reskilling “The AI-Ready Workforce Report” provides resources (framework model industry profiles readiness blueprint) to help workers/institutions “prepare for AI’s impact on jobs” (Featured Projects ? Report lines 88–92).
cse4_name: Generative AI  and Labor: Power Hype and Value at Work
cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse4_contributions: Labour Protections
cse4_justif: The primer published by Data & Society Research Institute supports labour protections by documenting how generative AI is linked to poor working conditions low wages/wage theft job insecurity and suppression of organizing for data workers and how outsourcing exploits gaps in labour protections (“Data Workers…” pp. 14–15). It also highlights risks of devaluation deskilling intensified work and increased competition calling for stronger rights/protections around worker data and working conditions (Conclusion pp. 23–24).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Request for Comment on Civil Rights Implications of the Federal Use of FacialRecognition Technology
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_justif: The American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) comment to the U.S. Commission on Civil Rights flags that federal facial recognition use can violate the Fourth Amendment right to privacy and enable mass surveillance (pp. 1–2; Part I.1; Part I.4).
cse2_name: Complaint and Request for Investigation Injunction and Other ReliefSubmitted byThe Electronic Privacy Information Center (EPIC)
cse2_type: Litigation or legal action.
cse2_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Safety and Security;  Data Sharing and Access;  Consumer Protection;  Access to Redress and Remedy;  Impact Assessments;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence
cse2_justif: The EPIC FTC complaint targets OpenAI’s privacy security and consumer harms: it alleges non-consensual web-scraping and retention of personal data plus sharing with third parties (IV.C pp. 12–14; IV.F pp. 20–22). It links these practices to safety/security failures and data leaks (IV.F pp. 20–22) and deceptive/consumer protection issues from “confabulations” and misuse (IV.D pp. 14–16; VI.B pp. 28–29). It seeks redress via injunction deletion/disgorgement consent and ongoing testing/audits/risk monitoring (II p.2; VI pp. 24–31; VII pp. 30–32) and to prevent misusing OpenAI’s tools to produce scams convincing consume-rmisinformation or other outputs in violation of the FTC Act (p. 30).
cse3_name: Disclosure and Transparency of Artificial Intelligence-Generated Content in PoliticalAdvertisements MB Docket 24-211
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_contributions: Safety and Security;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence
cse3_justif: On behalf of The Leadership Conference on Civil and Human Rights (The Leadership Conference) Common Cause (in coalition) urged the FCC to update rules to require clear disclosure/transparency when political content is AI-generated to reduce deceptive “deepfake” campaign material and related election harms (p. 1). This directly targets AI-facilitated misinformation by making synthetic political communications identifiable (Common Cause press release 4 Sep 2024) while addressing safety and security concerns in the political sector and during elections noting the potential risks to democracy and national securitystemming from malicious use of the technology (p. 1).
cse4_name: Synthetic Content. Exploring the Risks Technical Approaches andRegulatory Responses
cse4_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse4_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Consumer Protection;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence;  Safety and Security
cse4_justif: The Future of Privacy Forum (FPF) report explains how synthetic content can drive AI-facilitated misinformation (e.g. election disinformation voter manipulation) (Section II.B.1 “Elections and politics” pp. 6–7) and how it enables fraud/scams affecting consumer protection (Section II.E “Financial synthetic content scams” pp. 8–9). It frames these harms within privacy & security risks (intro to harms “in the realm of privacy and security” p. 5) and reviews U.S. regulatory responses (Appendix “Regulatory Frameworks in the U.S.” pp. 26–35) while outlining techniques for maintaining privacy and security for digital content transparency (p. 24).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: Contributions from Data Uruguay to the development of thereport provided for in art. 74 of Law No. 20.212
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination
cse1_justif: Data submitted recommendations to AGESIC's closed consultation under Art. 74 of Law 20.212 advocating for regulation of automated decision-making in public administration (p.1) and for incorporation of modern standards including algorithmic transparency and meaningful human oversight (p.4). The submission also called for equitable access to AI benefits and strengthened institutional capacity on AI and human rights. Date confirmed as June 20 2024 per the Datysoc blog post publishing both submissions: https://datysoc.org/2024/06/20/recomendaciones-para-la-regulacion-de-la-inteligencia-artificial-en-uruguay/
cse2_name: Workshops for ICD human rights defenders
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Environmental Impact;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse2_justif: Datysoc delivered a workshop module for ICD human rights defenders titled 'A Critical Look at Artificial Intelligence.' Session 1 covered AI and Human Rights including: transparency vs. opacity (black box systems) fairness and non-discrimination (algorithmic bias) autonomy (right to explainability) and sustainability (environmental impact). See slide 27 of the presentation: https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/1EN9-CCGNYVV1xZvSMK25NKORPZJ0rVmwgubFrFUDtPo/edit?slide=id.g36626520e4b_0_312#slide=id.g36626520e4b_0_312

cse1_name: “AI” Artificial Intelligence and its impact on work
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_contributions: Labour Protections;  Reskilling and upskilling initiatives
cse1_justif: The document was elaborated by the Cuesta Duarte Institute the center for studies of the main Union of Workers in Uruguay (PIT-CNT). It emphasizes that “the perspective of workers and their organizations is very important and is a voice that is missing in the debate on regulation” and that unions must defend “jobs working conditions mental health ethical issues participation and collective bargaining in technological incorporation discrimination and also the redistribution of the enormous productivity gains” (p. 3). It outlines main challenges for workers such as the need for protection against algorithmic biases and  discrimination privacy mental health and social justice (P. 10-11). About Upskilling (P.15)

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Contributions from Data Uruguay to the development of the report provided for in art. 74 of Law No. 20.212
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Consumer Protection;  Impact Assessments
cse1_justif: The document dated 20/06/2024 according to DatySocs blog page highlights the right to challenge automated decisions & calls for updating Law 18.331 to include algorithmic transparency & meaningful human oversight standards; proposes that AGESIC mandate impact assessments prior to public sector AI procurement in high-risk sectors such as health public security & education; advocates for mandatory labelling of synthetic content; & urgently calls for legal regulation of AI-powered surveillance technologies deployed by the Ministry of Interior including biometric identification systems and public microphones which currently operate without adequate legal safeguards under international human rights standards.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: National Forum Media Ayvon 2024
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_contributions: Human Oversight and Determination;  Fairness and Non Discrimination;  Transparency and Explainability
cse1_justif: The annual NGO-led National Forum “Media Ayvon” (2024/2025) organized by the Center for the Development of Modern Journalism of Uzbekistan convenes Parliament ministries experts academia NGOs and journalists to discuss law gender media literacy and AI. Its agenda tackles ethical and trust issues—including AI’s impact on journalism and fact-checking standards—fostering shared ethical rules. As a CSO initiative it exemplifies civil society engagement in shaping AI policy for media and journalists as well as responsible use of AI implementation of transparency rules  for media and human rights oversight.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: Tech Innovation Girls Uzbekistan 2025
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_justif: The Technovation Girls Uzbekistan program by Tech4Impact promotes inclusion and diversity in AI by empowering girls (ages 10–18) to develop AI-based solutions addressing social challenges. Through mentorship and training in coding AI and entrepreneurship it builds digital and leadership skills among young women reducing the gender gap in STEM and AI fields. The IT Education Association complements this by advancing digital literacy and inclusive IT education through nationwide training and forums such as ICTWeek Uzbekistan 2025.

cse1_name: Seminar 'Developing and using trustworthy artificial intelligence'
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Environmental Impact;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse1_justif: The seminar 'Developing and using trustworthy artificial intelligence' contributes to building policies towards sustainable transparent and responsible AI development. It shares best international practices on policies and regulations on responsible AI especially with regards to transparent data market regulations. It also addresses fairness transparency and explainability and environmental impact (sustainability) in their slides (p5). https://drive.google.com/file/d/14sDsPQx7AG9YTtmyDSWbV853lsXwahIE/view?usp=sharing
cse2_name: Report “Rapid assessment of artificial intelligence applications in state agencies in Vietnam: Policy recommendations”
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Environmental Impact;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse2_justif: The research findings shared in the report referred to human ethical standards such as safety accountability transparency and inclusion. It also raised the importance of transparency procurement and investment initiatives in the public sector. Their research report (p19) recommends environmental sustainability of AI ecosystems by optimizing resource use using clean energy for data centers & adopting green standards. Page 31 addresses fairness and non-discrimination by sharing 'Risks to fairness and equality arise because AI systems can lead to biases due to data training processes AI developers/deployers and decision-making processes'.
cse3_name: 'Digital Communications Code on Responsible Use of AI'
cse3_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse3_justif: The Vietnam Digital Communications Association (VDCA)  assigned the Institute for Policy Research and Media Development (IPS) to draft the Digital Communications Code on Responsible Use of AI (abbreviated as DCC). The Code includes 6 core principles (transparency trustworthiness respect for intellectual property rights protection of privacy respect for human dignity and rights and responsibility) and 29 specific guidelines from the above 6 principles for users of AI to create content along with 5 notes specifically for those who advertise in the digital environment.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: Report “Rapid assessment of artificial intelligence applications in state agencies in Vietnam: Policy recommendations”
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Gender Equality;  Children’s Rights;  Rights of Persons with Disabilities;  Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse1_justif: This report recommended that (1) the national strategy on AI consult the views & opinions and ensuring the rights and interests of different groups in AI policies and laws esp vulnerable groups such as people with disabilities ethnic minorities the poor women and children (3.1.2 p17); (2) AI applications should aim to benefit citizens; paying attention to the goal of ensuring sustainable developments with AI solutions that can support people with disabilities and ethnic minorities in providing public services and government-citizen interaction & using AI tools to review and analyze policies and legal regulations from a gender perspective preventing and responding to gender-based violence in the digital environment.  3.2.3.(p32)

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: Report on 'Artificial intelligence and employment in Vietnam: Opportunities challenges and some key policy recommendations'
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_contributions: Labour Protections;  Reskilling and upskilling initiatives;  AI Literacy
cse1_justif: The report published by the think tank Vietnam Institute for Policy Studies and Media Development (IPS) provided recommendations (Section 3 pages 9-12) on: 1) on labor protection (3.1 p9) - There needs to be a policy to support employers to maintain employment and ensure jobs for workers who have lost their jobs due to digital technology; 2) Reskilling - Vietnam's Employment Law needs to be amended to include policies to enhance the capacity of workers negatively impacted by digital technology in general and AI in particular especially those in vulnerable sectors such as manufacturing and agriculture(3.2 p10-12).

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Seminar on “Analyzing the landscape of artificial intelligence in the public sector in Vietnam”
cse1_type: Developing ethical guidelines principles or best practices.
cse1_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Impact Assessments
cse1_justif: The event provided an in-depth assessment of the current AI development landscape strategic direction and current status of AI applications in the public sector from there make policy recommendations to promote max potential of AI. Key Recommendations:1) Establish a clear legal framework and mechanism including regulations on accountability ethical standards risk management and data privacy protection. 2)Focus on AI ethics and risk management through monitoring systems impact assessments ensuring transparency fairness and privacy protection. IPS is an independent not-for-profit think tank: https://ips.org.vn/en/about-us.html. It is a member of the Vietnam Digital Communications Association a business association.
cse2_name: Seminar 'Developing and using trustworthy artificial intelligence'
cse2_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse2_contributions: Safety and Security;  Data Protection and Privacy;  Data Sharing and Access
cse2_justif: The roundtable was led by the Institute of Policy Studies (IPS) Vietnam. It addressed Data Protection and Privacy by highlighting the need for responsible data governance frameworks to support trustworthy AI development. It addressed Data Sharing and Access through discussions on strengthening data infrastructure and regulatory conditions that enable AI innovation. It also addressed Safety and Security by emphasizing risk management and the importance of legal safeguards to prevent harmful AI uses. The discussion focused on policy-level governance rather than specific technical measures.
cse3_name: Workshop 'Digital Communications Code on Responsible Use of AI'
cse3_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse3_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence;  Consumer Protection
cse3_justif: The Code of Responsible AI Use in Digital Communication was issued by the Vietnam Digital Communications Association (CSO) and establishes multiple core principles and practices. It addresses Data Protection and Privacy by requiring lawful necessary and transparent handling of personal data in AI-supported digital content. It addresses Consumer Protection by promoting disclosure of AI-generated materials accountability in content production and safeguards against misleading or manipulative uses of AI in media. The Code establishes ethical principles to guide responsible AI deployment in digital communication focusing on transparency and user trust.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
Civil Society Engagement in Inclusion and Diversity
cse1_name: We Digit Training Programme
cse1_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse1_justif: The initiative promotes gender equality by intentionally partnering with the Women’s Entrepreneurship Access Centre (WEAC) and prioritizing women’s participation. Out of the 175 youth participants nearly half were women as noted in the article reinforcing gender-inclusive leadership in tech education. In addition the training placed a strong emphasis on digital technologies including artificial intelligence equipping participants with practical hands-on skills. This focus supports inclusive AI literacy and empowers underrepresented groups especially women to access opportunities in the digital economy.
cse2_name: Survey Report by Internews Network Zambia on Artificial Intelligence Adoption by Civil Society Organizations in Zambia
cse2_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse2_contributions: Cultural and Linguistic Diversity
cse2_justif: A survey was conducted by Internews Network Zambia to report on Artificial Intelligence adoption by CSOs in Zambia. According to the survey findings on page two “Some CSOs cited that the AI tools they are utilizing do not have correct or ample information on Zambia and most of the AI tools are not tailored to suit the unique circumstances of their communities; ‘It’s not made for us’” says a participant from Livingstone. This entails that due to cultural diversity it is difficult for certain people to use AI. Linguistic diversity as cited on the same page equally makes it a challenge: “CSOs feel pressured to train AI chatbots to understand their local languages for free while most of these chatbots are for profit.”

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Labour and Skills
cse1_name: Panos Institute Hosts Information Session on AI and Civic Participation
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_justif: The session highlighted CSOs’ central role in promoting responsible AI adoption through community awareness advocacy for inclusion and digital literacy initiatives. By engaging government media and business stakeholders it fosters collaboration to build an ethical and inclusive AI ecosystem. Participants also discussed AI’s potential to drive entrepreneurship and job creation for youth and women across sectors like agriculture healthcare fintech and e-commerce equipping communities with knowledge and skills to understand and engage with AI thus strengthening AI literacy.
cse2_name: Artificial Intelligence (AI) Adoption by Civil Society Organizations (CSOs)  in Zambia A Survey Report
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Reskilling and upskilling initiatives
cse2_justif: The Internews Zambia survey report addresses AI literacy and reskilling/upskilling by analysing how Zambian CSOs use AI their knowledge gaps and capacity needs. The survey strengthens AI literacy and skills development by revealing how limited capacity affects CSO adoption of AI. The findings show that 54% of CSOs have not integrated AI and most lack training and resources. The Survey Overview emphasizes that “ethical considerations technical challenges and financial constraints pose significant barriers” highlighting the need for targeted skills support.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Trust and Safety
cse1_name: Media Awareness and Advocacy Raising on Responsible AI
cse1_type: Public engagement & awareness: conferences public awareness events/campaigns public consultations.
cse1_contributions: AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence;  Data Protection and Privacy;  Safety and Security
cse1_justif: The initiative promotes responsible AI use by media and CSOs to inform communities and strengthen democratic processes enhancing data sharing and access for public good. It also emphasizes safety and security by cautioning against misuse that could distort public discourse. Moreover it addresses AI-facilitated misinformation and violence by urging ethical AI practices to prevent manipulation and protect human rights. This is highlighted in Mr. Sifile’s call for objective responsible AI use to protect democracy from Panos Institute Southern Zambia.

indicator: Civil Society Engagement in Ethics and Sustainability
cse1_name: IndabaX 2024 Zimbabwe AI Symposium
cse1_type: Capacity building and collaboration: training courses workshops networks.
cse1_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse1_justif: Based on the conference's stated themes of Innovation Inclusion and Responsible AI the discussions provided clear evidence that Fairness and Discrimination were addressed. The outcomes directly noted a focus on building AI free from bias and discrimination and the inclusive multi-stakeholder nature of the event underscored this goal. Regarding Transparency and Explainability the program featured a dedicated practical session on Explainable AI (XAI). This hands-on workshop provided attendees with direct knowledge of methods to make AI decision-making processes understandable a core tenet of transparency framed within the broader push for responsible and accountable AI systems.
cse2_name: Al Report on Southern Africa
cse2_type: Knowledge generation: research technical reports.
cse2_contributions: Transparency and Explainability;  Human Oversight and Determination;  Environmental Impact;  Fairness and Non Discrimination
cse2_justif: On transparency the document discusses public disclosure of AI systems (pp. 71–72) and documents how biased data can foster discrimination against women and minorities (pp. 44–45).On human oversight it notes the need for independent regulators and redress mechanisms (pp. 59 71). On the environmental dimension citing sustainable data-centre plans alongside Africa's limited computing capacity (p. 45).

cse1_name: 6th Privacy Symposium Africa (PSA) Report - Key Policy-Recommendations-
cse1_type: Participation in public policy formulation implementation M&E: as part of advisory councils partnerships with state independent observers participating in parliamentary hearings.
cse1_contributions: Data Protection and Privacy;  Data Sharing and Access;  Safety and Security;  Impact Assessments;  AI-Facilitated Misinformation and Violence;  Consumer Protection;  Access to Redress and Remedy
cse1_justif: The Privacy Africa Sympsoium (PSA) and subsequently published report by CSO Unwanted Witness outlines several key policy recommendations to address the complexities of Artificial Intelligence (pp. 3 10 17). It explicitly explored AI in relation to data protection and privacy (p. 3); AI's unrestricted access to data (p. 18); Consumer trust and protection (pp. 6 15); combating misinformation (p.14); promote Collaboration for Data Security Innovation (p. 8); and note that new frameworks must incorporate ethical principlesrisk assessment mechanisms and provisions for algorithmic transparency and accountability (p. 10) and recommends to notify consumers about breaches and remediation steps (p.16) while noting current gaps for doing so (p.5)
